issue_title: the installer will only work on python 3.5 32 bit unless the following is fixed
issue_body:
in core.py, line 21, you have the following:
rt = ctypes.PyDLL('libmgrs.cp35-win32.pyd')

this failed on my python 3.6 32 bit. I ended up changing it to this to get it to work:
rt = ctypes.PyDLL('libmgrs.cp36-win32.pyd')

The file itself is located in:
<python root directory>\Lib\site-packages\libmgrs.cp36-win32.pyd

I figure there should be some code that generates the libmgrs pyd file name based on the version of python installed and if it's 32 bit vs 64 bit. I don't know how to do this, but I figured I'd post this here incase someone knows how to and is willing to do so.

issue_title: Comment in medpy.metric.binary.hd regarding connectivity is not correct
issue_body:
"connectivity : int
The neighbourhood/connectivity considered when determining the surface of the binary objects. This value is passed to scipy.ndimage.morphology.generate_binary_structure and should usually be >1>1. **Presumably does not influence the result in the case of the Hausdorff distance.**"
I have tested in 3D objects and it actually has a big influence in the result.

issue_title: Suggestion: Deleting breakpoint from breakpoints pane should not change view in code panel
issue_body:
##### Describe the issue
Deleting a breakpoint from the breakpoints pane takes you to that location in the code panel. In my mind that is disruptive since I don't really care about debugging that code any more, so seems pointless to jump back to it. Deleting a breakpoint shouldn't change the view in the code window. Or at least, should be a configurable option. 

##### Steps to replicate
Set a breakpoint in file x.cpp
Load file y.cpp so you're viewing it in the code panel
In breakpoints panel delete the breakpoint in x.cpp
x.cpp gets loaded in code panel, at point where deleted breakpoint existed

##### Describe your environment
Ubuntu 17.10, gdbgui 0.11.0.0 executable, gdb 8.0.1

issue_title: Weird pylint format / default with all info
issue_body:
What is the "pylint" format based on?

The old default for pylint itself was "pylint --msg-template='{msg_id}:{line:3d},{column}: {obj}: {msg}'" and now is "{C}:{line:3d},{column:2d}: {msg} ({symbol})" (https://docs.pylint.org/en/1.6.0/output.html).

I would like to use pylama's pylint format, but it is missing the column.

```python
    pattern = "%(filename)s:%(lnum)s:%(col)s: %(text)s"
    if options.format == 'pylint':
        pattern = "%(filename)s:%(lnum)s: [%(type)s] %(text)s"
```

What do you think about specifying `--pattern`?
And what about a default format that includes all information? (with type and col) :)

issue_title: EventsState should handle task-sent event from the client (not worker) correctly
issue_body:
If the directive CELERY_SEND_TASK_SENT_EVENT was enabled, the client (like the beat) will also send events (task-sent), and the event details are different from the workers'

In this case, EventsState.event() should check if the events received are from workers. Otherwise the latter render of dashboard page would failed due to the mismatched event details.

File events.py, Line 42, 
```python
self.counter[worker_name][event_type] += 1
```

should be changed to 
```python
if event_type != 'task-sent':
    self.counter[worker_name][event_type] += 1
```

issue_title: pubsub (at least) doesn't work with python 3
issue_body:

``` python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# -*- encoding: utf-8 -*-

from twisted.application import service
from txzmq import ZmqFactory, ZmqEndpoint, ZmqPubConnection
from twisted.internet import reactor
import time

class ZmqPubSubService(service.Service):
    def __init__(self):
        self.zf = ZmqFactory()


    def publish(self):
        data = str(time.time()).encode('ascii')
        print("Publishing {}".format(data))
        self.subconn.publish(data, b'test')
        reactor.callLater(1, self.publish)


    def startService(self):
        self.sube = ZmqEndpoint('bind', 'tcp://127.0.0.1:5557')
        self.push = ZmqEndpoint('bind', 'tcp://127.0.0.1:5558')
        self.subconn = ZmqPubConnection(self.zf, self.sube)
        self.publish()


    def stopService(self):
        del self.sube
        del self.push
        del self.subconn
        #return self.tcpService.stopService()
```

```
2016-01-09 21:13:36+0100 [-]   File "zmq/utils/buffers.pxd", line 151, in buffers.asbuffer (zmq/backend/cython/socket.c:7985)
2016-01-09 21:13:36+0100 [-] TypeError: 116 does not provide a buffer interface.
```

The reason is this code:

tyzmq/connection.py
``` python
    def send(self, message):
        """
        Send message via ZeroMQ socket.

        Sending is performed directly to ZeroMQ without queueing. If HWM is
        reached on ZeroMQ side, sending operation is aborted with exception
        from ZeroMQ (EAGAIN).

        After writing read is scheduled as ZeroMQ may not signal incoming
        messages after we touched socket with write request.

        :param message: message data, could be either list of str (multipart
            message) or just str
        :type message: str or list of str
        """
        if not hasattr(message, '__iter__'):
            self.socket.send(message, constants.NOBLOCK)
        else:
            for m in message[:-1]:
                self.socket.send(m, constants.NOBLOCK | constants.SNDMORE)
            self.socket.send(message[-1], constants.NOBLOCK)
```

In python3 bytes and str both have `__iter__` methods, so this code enters the wrong path (the one where a multipart message should be sent).
The solution should be something like this, which also works in python2 where bytes==str:

``` python
        if type(message) is bytes:
            self.socket.send(message, constants.NOBLOCK)
        elif hasattr(message, '__iter__'):
            for m in message[:-1]:
                self.socket.send(m, constants.NOBLOCK | constants.SNDMORE)
            self.socket.send(message[-1], constants.NOBLOCK)
```

Maybe even an else with a raise could be preferred like this:

``` python
        else:
            raise Exception('Only an iterable of bytes or type bytes is allowed as argument.')
```

issue_title: Performance optimization of metadata loading
issue_body:
The `load_metadata` function that makes the Project Gutenberg meta-data RDF graph available to the meta-data extractors takes for ever to run as it is loading ~130MB of data into memory from a flat file (~850MB uncompressed).

A low-cost way to address this is to investigate [database-backed stores for the RDF graph](http://rdflib.readthedocs.org/en/latest/persistence.html) instead of loading it all into memory.

Alternative ways to tackle the issue include sharding the meta-data file along multiple dimensions (e.g. text identifier, author, etc.) but this would require adding code to create the pertinent shards to every MetadataExtractor i.e., extending the library involves more work in the future.

issue_title: Bad update with an user
issue_body:
Hi! I found an error this morning, that its quite annoying:

Suppose you've got an user with two or more organizations, and you need to append a tag to the list tags of the user so your code would look something like this:

    USER_ID = 1234
    creds = {...}
    zenpy_client = Zenpy(**creds)
    user = zenpy.users(id=USER_ID)
    user.tags.append("new_tag")    #this is the tag you need to append
    zenpy.users.update(user)          #send the user to zendesk

And the expected result is that your user now has the new tag, but what happens is that the user, loose all his organizations except of one (the default_one).
If you do a requests call (with the requests package of python, for exemple), and the payload you need to send will look like this: {"user": "tags": ["old_tag", "new_tag"]} simulating an append, then you made the PUT method, and know you will see that your user is update successfully and does not loose any organization.

So to implement this with Zenpy, will be like this:
    
    from zenpy.lib.api_objects import User
    ....
    USER_ID = 1234
    creds = {...}
    zenpy_client = Zenpy(**creds)
    user = zenpy.users(id=USER_ID)
    tags = user.tags.append("new_tag")    #this is the tag you need to append
    zenpy.users.update(User(id=user.id, tags=tags))          #send the user to zendesk

This is a current bug, in many zendesk framework, less in zendesk ruby framework (their official), analyzing it, I detect that the framework, just send to an update method, just the modified fields or attributes of the user.

It would be nice to have this feature, @facetoe

```python >>> el = PyQuery('') >>> el.find('b:lt(1)') [, , ] >>> el.find('b:first') [, , ] >>> el.find('b:last') [, , ] >>> el.find('b:eq(1)') [] >>> el.find('b:lt(1)') [, , ] >>> el.find('b:gt(1)') [] ``` I started poking at the xpath stuff but was quickly overwhelmed :) Here's a patch for the start of a test: ```diff diff --git a/tests/test_pyquery.py b/tests/test_pyquery.py index 6cbaf50..49dca45 100644 --- a/tests/test_pyquery.py +++ b/tests/test_pyquery.py @@ -205,6 +205,21 @@ class TestSelector(TestCase): assert len(e(":empty")) == 1 assert len(e(":contains('Heading')")) == 8 + def test_indexing_pseudo_classes(self): + e = pq('') + + first = e.find('b:first') + self.assertEqual(len(first), 1) + self.assertIn('c', first[0].attrib) + + index_1 = e.find('b:eq(1)') + self.assertEqual(len(index_1), 1) + self.assertIn('d', index_1[0].attrib) + + last = e.find('b:last') + self.assertEqual(len(last), 1) + self.assertIn('e', last[0].attrib) + def test_on_the_fly_dom_creation(self): e = self.klass(self.html) assert e('

Hello world

').text() == 'Hello world' ```

issue_title: Reading a pdf from file like object or data not working in python 3 with bytesIO
issue_body:
I have noticed that it is possible to make a `PdfReader` either by specifying a filename or file-like object, or by giving the data directly with fdata argument. This is great, however, it doesn't work if I give it a `BytesIO` object since the various functions in the following code only work with strings. False

issue_title: Surface-surface intersection fails on "almost" parallel lines
issue_body:
```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> nodes1 = np.asfortranarray([
...     [1.625 , 1.1875 ],
...     [1.5   , 1.03125],
...     [1.375 , 0.875  ],
...     [1.6875, 1.0    ],
...     [1.5625, 0.84375],
...     [1.75  , 0.8125 ],
... ])
>>> nodes2 = np.asfortranarray([
...     [1.25     , 0.75     ],
...     [1.34375  , 0.78125  ],
...     [1.4375   , 0.8125   ],
...     [1.375    , 0.875    ],
...     [1.4609375, 0.9140625],
...     [1.484375 , 1.015625 ],
... ])
>>> import bezier
>>> S1 = bezier.Surface(nodes1, degree=2, _copy=False)
>>> S2 = bezier.Surface(nodes2, degree=2, _copy=False)
>>> import seaborn
>>> ax = S1.plot(256)
>>> _ = S2.plot(256, ax=ax)
>>> _ = ax.axis('scaled')
>>> ax.figure.show()
>>> S1.intersect(S2)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "bezier/surface.py", line 1144, in intersect
    edge_pairs)
  File "bezier/_intersection_helpers.py", line 1437, in all_intersections
    raise NotImplementedError(msg)
NotImplementedError: The number of candidate intersections is too high.
17 accepted pairs gives 68 candidate pairs.
```

![figure_1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/520669/22129085/d8ff08c8-de58-11e6-83d0-3937d22fa9bd.png)

This actually comes from a single edge-pair (I originally assumed the issue was that the bound of 16 for two curves was overly restrictive for multiple curves, relaxing to 20 allows this to pass BTW)

```python
>>> for i, edge1 in enumerate(S1.edges):
...     for j, edge2 in enumerate(S2.edges):
...         try:
...             I = edge1.intersect(edge2)
...             print('{}, {} success'.format(i, j))
...         except NotImplementedError:
...             print('{}, {} failure'.format(i, j))
... 
0, 0 success
0, 1 success
0, 2 failure
1, 0 success
1, 1 success
1, 2 success
2, 0 success
2, 1 success
2, 2 success
```

So

```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> nodes1 = np.asfortranarray([
...     [1.625 , 1.1875 ],
...     [1.5   , 1.03125],
...     [1.375 , 0.875  ],
... ])
>>> nodes2 = np.asfortranarray([
...     [1.484375, 1.015625],
...     [1.375   , 0.875   ],
...     [1.25    , 0.75    ],
... ])
>>> import bezier
>>> C1 = bezier.Curve(nodes1, degree=2, _copy=False)
>>> C2 = bezier.Curve(nodes2, degree=2, _copy=False)
>>> import seaborn
>>> ax = C1.plot(256)
>>> _ = C2.plot(256, ax=ax)
>>> _ = ax.axis('scaled')
>>> ax.figure.show()
>>> C1.intersect(C2)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "bezier/curve.py", line 583, in intersect
    intersections = _intersection_helpers.all_intersections(candidates)
  File "bezier/_intersection_helpers.py", line 1437, in all_intersections
    raise NotImplementedError(msg)
NotImplementedError: The number of candidate intersections is too high.
17 accepted pairs gives 68 candidate pairs.
```

and the crazy thing is, they never intersect!

![figure_1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/520669/22129275/cf96eb7e-de59-11e6-9d28-4c63aaaf90eb.png)

Via [implicitization][1], this is straightforward to dismiss:

```
Computed f(t) = f1(x2(t), y2(t)) =
    (-0.0009765625) * t^0 + (0.001953125) * t^1 + (-0.0087890625) * t^2
======
Resultant(f, f') = -5.78476 (computed with L2-normalized ||f|| = 1 on [0, 1])
Roots:
```

This `f(t)` is just `9 t^2 - 2 t + 1` divided by `-1024`. The unscaled version has discriminant equal to `(-2)^2 - 4(9)(1) = -32`, so it has no real roots.

```python
>>> from bezier._intersection_helpers import IntersectionStrategy
>>> I, = S1.intersect(S2, strategy=IntersectionStrategy.algebraic)
>>> I
<CurvedPolygon (num_sides=3)>
>>>
>>> ax = S1.plot(256)
>>> _ = S2.plot(256, ax=ax)
>>> _ = I.plot(256, ax=ax)
>>> _ = ax.axis('scaled')
>>> ax.figure.show()
```

![figure_1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/520669/26339484/1effa892-3f3d-11e7-9108-6d805a8c37c3.png)

[1]: https://gist.github.com/dhermes/29f3c98c87d91bc0f41349915142e3d7/fa17c7eed24e86ad41116a84e8114e0d7b80627f#file-output-txt-L239-L253

----

```
09550abcf0efeb239b17f9871ccac93fcd1bcd4a
$ # UPDATED: Still b0rken
$ git log -1 --pretty=%H
```

issue_title: Suspected memory leak with pysnmp==4.4.1 on PyPy 5.9 only (Python seems fine)
issue_body:
The code to reproduce is fairly simple- obviously you'll need PyPy 5.9 installed first as well as pysnmp==4.4.1.

`
    from pysnmp.hlapi import SnmpEngine, UdpTransportTarget, ContextData, ObjectType, ObjectIdentity, getCmd, UsmUserData, usmHMACSHAAuthProtocol, usmAesCfb128Protocol

    ITERATIONS = 65536

    for i in range(0, ITERATIONS):
        snmp_engine = SnmpEngine()
        context_data = ContextData()

        errorIndication, errorStatus, errorIndex, varBinds = next(
            getCmd(
                snmp_engine,
                UsmUserData(
                    userName='some_username',
                    authKey='some_auth_key',
                    privKey='some_priv_key,
                    authProtocol=usmHMACSHAAuthProtocol,
                    privProtocol=usmAesCfb128Protocol,
                ),
                UdpTransportTarget(
                    ('some_host', 161),
                    timeout=5,
                    retries=2,
                ),
                context_data,
                ObjectType(ObjectIdentity('.1.3.6.1.2.1.1.5.0'))
            )
        )

        print '{0}/{1} - {2}'.format(i + 1, ITERATIONS, varBinds)

        del snmp_engine
        del context_data
`

Under PyPy 5.9 memory use steadily increases until (in my experience) MacOS crashes; in Python however, all seems fine,

I'll completely understand if this issue is immediately closed because probably the data suggests it's a PyPy problem rather than a Python problem; I just figured it might be useful data to be aware of.

I'll also raise it on the PyPy Bitbucket.

issue_title: Tests failing randomly
issue_body:
The tests are failing since the introduction of the `--progress` support. The issue being inconsistent failing of the same code between calls. Cf the [travis reports](https://travis-ci.org/guyzmo/git-repo/builds/129130635).

With a fair bit of debugging, I found out the issue is that because GitPython relies on the `kwargs` construct to pass around optional arguments, the order of argument will depend on the order of the items within the dict. As when I introduced progress support, `opt_arg` contains `'-v'` and `'--progress'` now, the order depending on both `hash('-v')` and `hash('--progress')`. To fix this, making the optional arguments ordered on their value instead on their hash will do the job, but sadly it's not something that can be fixed with a tiny bit of monkey patching…

Thus, I've opened a PR upstream at gitpython-developers/GitPython#431 with a very simple fix for that.

issue_title: Telnet Session Closed by find_prompt() after call to is_alive().
issue_body:
When ```is_alive()``` is called to test a connection, it sends '0xFF,0xF1' to the host (telnet 'Interpret as Command' followed by 'NOP').  
The host (in this test case, a Cisco 2911 IOS v15.4(3)M3 ) responds with '0x07' (BELL) and '0x71' (letter 'q').
When ```find_prompt()``` is subsequently called, it sends '0x0a' ('\n').  As the host previously responded to the IAC+NOP with the letter 'q', the letter 'q' is on the command line.  When ```find_prompt()``` sends a '\n', it executes the 'q' command (short for 'quit' on IOS) and the host closes the connection.

**Steps to reproduce:**
1. Establish connection to host via telnet
2. Call ```is_alive()```
3. Call ```find_prompt()```


```console
DEBUG:netmiko:read_channel: q

Traceback (most recent call last):
...
  File ".\miniconda3\lib\site-packages\netmiko\base_connection.py", line 823, in find_prompt
    self.clear_buffer()
  File ".\miniconda3\lib\site-packages\netmiko\base_connection.py", line 828, in clear_buffer
    self.read_channel()
  File ".\miniconda3\lib\site-packages\netmiko\base_connection.py", line 329, in read_channel
    output = self._read_channel()
  File ".\miniconda3\lib\site-packages\netmiko\base_connection.py", line 316, in _read_channel
    output = self.remote_conn.read_very_eager().decode('utf-8', 'ignore')
  File ".\miniconda3\lib\telnetlib.py", line 366, in read_very_eager
    return self.read_very_lazy()
  File ".\miniconda3\lib\telnetlib.py", line 403, in read_very_lazy
    raise EOFError('telnet connection closed')
EOFError: telnet connection closed
```

Wireshark Follow TCP Stream Hex Dump:
```
    0000190D  0a 65 6e 64 0d 0a 0d 0a  74 77 73 73 63 2d 72 74   .end.... twssc-rt
    0000191D  72 23                                              r#
# Netmiko is_alive() sends IAC-NOP
0000006A  ff ff f1                                           ...
# Host responds with 'BELL' and 'q'
    0000191F  07                                                 .
    00001920  71                                                 q
# Netmiko find_prompt() sends '\n'
0000006D  0a                                                 .
    00001921  0d 0a                                              ..
```

This behavior appeared in v2.1.0, after the following change was made in ```is_alive()``` (commit [2a0ce80](https://github.com/ktbyers/netmiko/commit/2a0ce80e2f9e64a495ae5a013ba1d2541c6187c8)):

```patch
--- A/netmiko-2.0.2/netmiko/base_connection.py	Fri Feb  9 12:55:10 2018
+++ B/netmiko-2.1.0/netmiko-2.1.0/netmiko/base_connection.py	Wed Mar  7 10:13:56 2018
@@ -283,7 +283,7 @@
                 # Try sending IAC + NOP (IAC is telnet way of sending command
                 # IAC = Interpret as Command (it comes before the NOP)
                 log.debug("Sending IAC + NOP")
-                self.device.write_channel(telnetlib.IAC + telnetlib.NOP)
+                self.write_channel(telnetlib.IAC + telnetlib.NOP)
                 return True
             except AttributeError:
                 return False
```

Prior to this change, every call to ```is_alive()``` for a telnet connection returned false.

If I change ```self.write_channel(telnetlib.IAC + telnetlib.NOP)``` to ```self.write_channel(' ')```, it works as expected, though this may not be a desired solution.

issue_title: HP procurve switch prep session
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm working on getting the configuration from HP procurve switch. However, what I just found is if the HP procruve switches are in the stack, there will be another screen that we need to skip. Here is an example:

 Stack Members

  SN MAC Address   System Name      Device Type           Status
  -- ------------- ---------------- --------------------- ----------------------
  0  xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx       ProCurve Switch      Commander Up
  1  yyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyy       ProCurve Switch      Member Up
Enter switch number to connect to or <CR>:

Basically, we need to send another '\n'. I've added it to hp_procurve_ssh.py to resolve the issue. An extra enter will not cause any other issue for normal switch but would help the stack.

Here is the code that I use, and it works fine for hp standalone switch and the stack.


    def session_preparation(self):
        """
        Prepare the session after the connection has been established.
        Procurve uses - 'Press any key to continue'
        """
        delay_factor = self.select_delay_factor(delay_factor=0)
        time.sleep(1 * delay_factor)
        self.write_channel("\n")
        time.sleep(1 * delay_factor)
        self.write_channel("\n")
        time.sleep(1 * delay_factor)

issue_title: Failed to enter configuration mode - slow device?
issue_body:
Hi,

I have one device that seems to take slightly longer to enter configuration mode.

Throws the following exception:
```
    net_connect.config_mode()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/netmiko/ssh_connection.py", line 49, in config_mode
    return super(SSHConnection, self).config_mode(config_command=config_command)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/netmiko/base_connection.py", line 438, in config_mode
    raise ValueError("Failed to enter configuration mode")
ValueError: Failed to enter configuration mode
```

Switch type is cisco_ios:
```
Switch Ports Model              SW Version            SW Image
------ ----- -----              ----------            ----------
*    1 28    WS-C3750G-24PS     15.0(2)SE4            C3750-IPSERVICESK9-M
```

Adding a few seconds sleep in the check_config_mode solves the issue for me. It seems netmiko is just checking too quickly for this particular device.
```python
    def check_config_mode(self, check_string=''):
        '''
        Checks if the device is in configuration mode or not

        Returns a boolean
        '''
        time.sleep(3)
        output = self.send_command('\n', strip_prompt=False, strip_command=False)
        if check_string in output:
            return True
        else:
            return False
```

I am not suggesting just sleeping for a constant time as a solution, rather is it possible to use a delay_factor like other methods to achieve the same thing? Or even just re-checking after a few seconds, if it fails once?

issue_title: unified Tor launcher/builder API
issue_body:

On top of that, it acts as a Factory/Builder for other interesting instances: TorState, TorConfig, client- and server- side endpoints, etc.

There is an early prototype in the "control" branch: https://github.com/meejah/txtorcon/tree/controller

issue_title: Pandas DataReader Errors??!!?
issue_body:
When trying to do a simple test and retrieve market data, I get an error something to the order of "change import pandas.io.data to import pandas_datareader". I believe this error comes from the fact that pandas has updated the way that the datareader is imported!

So "ffn" is unusable with the current update of pandas. To fix this, I navigated to the "data.py" file in the ffn folder of python and changed line 4 to "import pandas_datareader.data as pdata" then changed line 113 to "return pdata.DataReader(name, **kwargs)". After that, everything works as it should!

Simple fix, thought I should leave a comment for anybody else who might stumble upon this!

issue_title: melody.freq_to_voicing returns single boolean if not a numpy array
issue_body:
[```melody.freq_to_voicing```](https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/master/mir_eval/melody.py#L144) is meant to take a numpy array. I accidentally discovered that if you instead give it a list of floats instead of a numpy array, it returns ```True```, rather than an array of booleans.

```
>>> np.array([440.0, 0.0, 0.0]) > 0
[True, False, False]
>>> [440.0, 0.0, 0.0] > 0
True
```
I definitely just used the function wrong, but it is a bit of a sinister error. I don't have any ideas for an ideal solution... maybe the function could first check that ```frequencies``` is indeed a numpy array and throw a ```ValueError``` if not? Any ideas? Happy to make a PR.

issue_title: No definitions found for certain scenario
issue_body:
Please see a detailed description and discussion in tkf/emacs-jedi#281, which turns out to be probably a Jedi issue. In brief:

1. Clone [this repository](https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr): `git clone https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr`
2. Go to [line 49](https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr/blob/master/aslam_offline_calibration/kalibr/python/kalibr_imu_camera_calibration/IccCalibrator.py#L49) and try to find definitions of `addDesignVariables`. No definitions are found, yet the `addDesignVariables` function is defined in [IccSensors.py](https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr/blob/master/aslam_offline_calibration/kalibr/python/kalibr_imu_camera_calibration/IccSensors.py#L316), which is a python file in the same directory.

Here is a Python console session showing the fault (courtesy [syohex](https://github.com/syohex) - which shows that I'm not the only one affected):
```
% ipython
Python 2.7.10 (default, Oct 23 2015, 19:19:21)
Type "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

IPython 5.1.0 -- An enhanced Interactive Python.
?         -> Introduction and overview of IPython's features.
%quickref -> Quick reference.
help      -> Python's own help system.
object?   -> Details about 'object', use 'object??' for extra details.

In [1]: import jedi

In [2]: source = open('IccCalibrator.py', 'r').read()

In [3]: script = jedi.Script(source, 49, len('        self.CameraChain.'), 'IccCalibrator.py')

In [4]: defs = script.goto_definitions()

In [5]: defs
Out[5]: []
```

I am running Ubuntu 14.04 and:
```
$ pip show jedi
---
Name: jedi
Version: 0.10.0
Location: /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages
Requires: 
$ pip show python
---
Name: Python
Version: 2.7
Location: /usr/lib/python2.7/lib-dynload
Requires: 
```

Since [line 177](https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr/blob/master/aslam_offline_calibration/kalibr/python/kalibr_calibrate_imu_camera#L177) calls the [registerCamChain](https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr/blob/master/aslam_offline_calibration/kalibr/python/kalibr_imu_camera_calibration/IccCalibrator.py#L58) function with `camChain` (an `IccCameraChain` object) argument, Jedi should make the guess that `self.CameraChain` is an `IccCameraChain` object and thus should be able to find the definition of `addDesignVariables` on [line 49](https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr/blob/master/aslam_offline_calibration/kalibr/python/kalibr_imu_camera_calibration/IccCalibrator.py#L49), which is a method of the `IccCameraChain` class.

issue_title: Spurious added = in completion candidates
issue_body:
Hello! I have seen a few weird cases where Jedi returns all possible completion candidates with an equals sign (`=`) as a suffix, apparently assuming those are possible keyword arguments to the called function. Sadly, this only happens in some files, and only sometimes – the same file can work perfectly fine usually, but break otherwise. I refrained from opening a bug report for a while due to this completely unhelpful description, but as I am now solving the problem by simply stripping equals signs, I figured I'd at least make you aware of this effect in case others report it as well.

(Only slightly) more information at jorgenschaefer/elpy#613

Typical example:

```Python
def foo(barbaz):
    return str(bar_|_
```

This will – under those unclear circumstances – provide a completion candidate of `barbaz=` instead of `barbaz`.

issue_title: Mac Available Memory might be slightly inaccurate
issue_body:
psutil's [virtual_memory on Mac](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/1b5f8d55d22167d640eeb52d0b82f69dec83d210/psutil/_psosx.py#L123) calculates `avail = inactive + free`. `free` comes from [psutil_virtual_mem](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/1b5f8d55d22167d640eeb52d0b82f69dec83d210/psutil/_psutil_osx.c#L684) and is calculated as `(vm_statistics_data_t.free_count - vm_statistics_data_t.speculative_count) * PAGE_SIZE`. This means that `avail = (vm_statistics_data_t.inactive_count + vm_statistics_data_t.free_count - vm_statistics_data_t.speculative_count) * PAGE_SIZE`. However, I can't find any evidence that `speculative_count` is effectively _unavailable_ memory. An [Apple engineer defines speculative_count](https://lists.apple.com/archives/darwin-kernel/2008/Jun/msg00001.html) as:

> on Mac OS X 10.5 we introduced a new, fifth category of memory, speculative memory, used to hold pages that have been read from disk speculatively

The way I interpret this is that the effective amount of free RAM is `free - speculative`; but, speculative, just by its definition, is probably something that is effectively available for program demands (just like inactive). So I think psutil is calculating `free` correctly, but `avail` incorrectly and `avail` should add back in `speculative`.

issue_title: Process.cpu_percent() should guard against cpu_count() returning None
issue_body: It's a corner case, but as per doc `cpu_count()` may return None. Currently `Process.cpu_percent()` does not take this into account and as such may raise TypeError.

issue_title: formule definition for convert euler angle to quaternion
issue_body:
Hello
I'm trying to use the function "from_euler_angles ()". By cons I have a problem because it does not leave the good resustat.

For example :

from_euler_angles (0,0, np.pi / 8) = from_euler_angles (np.pi / 8,0,0) which is not true.

You use this formula : 

    R[..., 0] = np.cos(alpha/2)*np.cos(beta/2)*np.cos(gamma/2) - np.sin(alpha/2)*np.cos(beta/2)*np.sin(gamma/2)
    R[..., 1] = np.cos(alpha/2)*np.sin(beta/2)*np.sin(gamma/2) - np.sin(alpha/2)*np.sin(beta/2)*np.cos(gamma/2)
    R[..., 2] = np.cos(alpha/2)*np.sin(beta/2)*np.cos(gamma/2) + np.sin(alpha/2)*np.sin(beta/2)*np.sin(gamma/2)
    R[..., 3] = np.sin(alpha/2)*np.cos(beta/2)*np.cos(gamma/2) + np.cos(alpha/2)*np.cos(beta/2)*np.sin(gamma/2)

For X,Y, Z rotation you must use :

    R[..., 0] = np.cos(alpha/2)*np.cos(beta/2)*np.cos(gamma/2) + np.sin(alpha/2)*np.sin(beta/2)*np.sin(gamma/2)
    R[..., 1] = np.sin(alpha/2)*np.cos(beta/2)*np.cos(gamma/2) - np.cos(alpha/2)*np.sin(beta/2)*np.sin(gamma/2)
    R[..., 2] = np.cos(alpha/2)*np.sin(beta/2)*np.cos(gamma/2) + np.sin(alpha/2)*np.cos(beta/2)*np.sin(gamma/2)
    R[..., 3] = np.cos(alpha/2)*np.cos(beta/2)*np.sin(gamma/2) - np.sin(alpha/2)*np.sin(beta/2)*np.cos(gamma/2)

ref : https://ntrs.nasa.gov/archive/nasa/casi.ntrs.nasa.gov/19770024290.pdf

issue_title: Argument to on_callback_query() is not a Message
issue_body:
While debugging a problem I had with the way I handled the callback query in my bot I discovered that the actual argument passed to the `on_callback_quey(self, msg)` named msg was in fact... not a Telegram Message 😱.

Two clues led me to this conclusion. First when trying to convert to a namedtuple `Message` I get a warning:
```python
>>> Message(msg)
/usr/lib/python3.5/site-packages/telepot/namedtuple.py:48: UserWarning: Unexpected fields: data, id, inline_message_id, chat_instance
Bot API seems to have added new fields to the returned data. This version of namedtuple is not able to capture them.

Please upgrade telepot by:
  sudo pip install telepot --upgrade

If you still see this message after upgrade, that means I am still working to bring the code up-to-date. Please try upgrade again a few days later. In the meantime, you can access the new fields the old-fashioned way, through the raw dictionary.
  warnings.warn(s, UserWarning)
```

Ok at first I did not paid too much attention to the error since Telegram has been updated yesterday with some new types related to games.

Second clue: for debugging purpose I always log a pprint of each message my bot receive. This one was no exception:
```python
>>> pprint(msg)
{'chat_instance': '-6597979017591450366',
 'data': '1 0',
 'from': {'first_name': 'James',
          'id': 1007,
          'last_name': 'Bond',
          'username': 'jamesbond007'},
 'id': '123456789012345',
 'inline_message_id': 'BAAAAJorAAAz4B6OldTqM5uGeY8'}
```
(ok, to protect Great Britain finest agent privacy I edited some values)

Searching through the Telegram API doc it seems the actual object received is a [CallbackQuery](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#callbackquery) dict.

If this is actually true, the `msg` name of the param is really misleading... it appears in the [source code](https://telepot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_modules/telepot/helper.html#DefaultRouterMixin) and in the [examples](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/blob/master/examples/callback/lover.py). I know that technically a argument name is not a bug... but since we mostly infer the role and type of an argument through its name I really think this could be misleading.

issue_title: (a+b+c) is not commutative and overestimates overlap
issue_body:
I have three BED Tools, a b and c, and I expect that (a+b+c).count() should == (b+a+c).count(), since that function is intended to extract features that are common to all three BedTools.  (as discussed here: http://packages.python.org/pybedtools/3-brief-examples.html#example-2-intersections-for-a-3-way-venn-diagram)  However, in my tests, I find conflicting results, depending on the order of the operands.  Also, the total number present in (a+b+c).count() [or any other order] seems to be an over-estimate of the actual number of overlaps, given that I find elements in (a+b+c) that are specific to only b.  I can provide BED files for testing if needed. 

as a consequence, "venn_mpl.py -c a.BED -a b.BED -b c.BED " gives a different result from "venn_mpl.py -a a.BED -b b.BED -c c.BED "

issue_title: re-add support for initializing sections with an explicit stream
issue_body:
somewhere between 0.24 and now, the Section constructors had their stream argument removed, and the stream is now grabbed from the reader. I would like the ability to pass in an explicit stream to use besides the one in the reader.

The rationale for this is that when trying to analyze binaries robustly, section data is not actually considered by loaders and is thus a potential wedge for malicious actors to confuse analysis tools by providing misleading data. Instead, we want to use just the program headers (segments) for loading. This means getting the string tables/relocations/etc out of the PT_DYNAMIC segment instead of the section table. However, entries in PT_DYNAMIC provide their addresses in terms of _how they look when actually mapped into an address space_. In our application, we're already constructing the loaded binary into an address space object which supports the python stream interface, so instead of trying to translate the address back into an offset into the binary by checking each of the PT_LOADs again, we used to be able to just provide our copy of the address space directly to a RelocationSection object to be able to read out the relocations, for example.

If you think this is an ok change, I can do it pretty easily.

issue_title: Неправильно обрабатывается кварг type == 'boolean' в конфиге вьюх
Abuse of `setup_requires` for the non-runtime dependency `pytest-runner`
issue_body:
The `setup.py` script has as a setup requirement a test-time one, `pytest-runner`.
According to [the docs](https://pip.readthedocs.io/en/1.4.1/cookbook.html#controlling-setup-requires), `setup_requires` is for dependencies of the setup-tools themselves.

Such requirements typically are placed in the `tests_require` argument.
If this is currently impossible due to some installation script, there is additionally the `test_suite` argument for facilitating the launching of tests.

Thank you for your time.

issue_title: Large coordinates are not returned exactly, because of conversion to floats
issue_body:
I've noticed that the coordinates of the paths returned by `pyclipper.Execute()` are represented using floats. This introduces some inaccuracies for very large coordinate values as not all values of an `int64_t` can be represented by a `double`. I've added a test case here showing the problem:

https://github.com/Feuermurmel/pyclipper/tree/inexact-coordinates

I see that the library supports automatic scaling of coordinate values by setting the global `pyclipper.SCALING_FACTOR`. This of course means that, unless this variable is set to 1, a type other than `int` is necessary to represent coordinates. Thus I'm not sure what the best way is to solve this problem. I currently see the following options:

– Remove `pyclipper.SCALING_FACTOR`, but there are probably some people using it.
– Only use floats when `pyclipper.SCALING_FACTOR` is not set to 1. Seems a bit hack-ish.
– Add a second implementation of `pyclipper.Pyclipper` which ignores the scaling factor and uses ints instead. This seems obscure and bloats the library.

issue_title: Error in Class Activation map for hidden layers
issue_body:
I was trying out the class activation map of some cat and dog images for different layers of VGG16 (using `keras.applications.vgg16`, not `vis.utils.vggnet`), and the code, shown in (https://raghakot.github.io/keras-vis/visualizations/attention/) does not seem to be working for layers other than 'predictions'. When I use `layer_name='block1_conv2'`, it is throwing an error, `ValueError: Unable to detect penultimate 'Convolution2D' or 'Pooling2D' layer for layer_idx: 2`. On the other hand, for `layer_name='block2_conv2'`, or `layer_name='block3_conv2'` there is an `AssertionError` for `len(item_slice) == 4`.

Further, when I am using `layer_name='predictions'`, the prediction is performed correctly for the cat and dog image (Tabby, and Siberian Husky), but the heat map is barely on the dog.
![keras-vis-cam-2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11420071/27305801-242d7e04-5511-11e7-9d6b-c35a0023087c.jpeg)

How can this be solved?

The code I am using is:
```
import numpy as np
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use('Agg')
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.axis as ax

from keras.preprocessing.image import img_to_array
from keras.applications.imagenet_utils import preprocess_input

from vis.utils import utils
from keras.applications.vgg16 import VGG16, decode_predictions
from vis.visualization import visualize_cam

# Build the VGG16 network with ImageNet weights
model = VGG16(weights='imagenet', include_top=True)
print('Model loaded.')

# The name of the layer we want to visualize
# (see model definition in vggnet.py)
layer_name = 'predictions' # 'block1_conv2', 'block2_conv2', 'block3_conv2'
layer_idx = [idx for idx, layer in enumerate(model.layers) if layer.name == layer_name][0]

# Images corresponding to cat, and dog
image_paths = [
    "cat.jpg",
    "dog.jpg"
]

heatmaps = []
for path in image_paths:
    seed_img = utils.load_img(path, target_size=(224, 224))
    x = np.expand_dims(img_to_array(seed_img), axis=0)
    x = preprocess_input(x)
    pred_class = np.argmax(model.predict(x))
    print('Predicted class is ' +str(decode_predictions(model.predict(x), top=1)[0][0]))
    # Here we are asking it to show attention such that prob of `pred_class` is maximized.
    heatmap = visualize_cam(model, layer_idx, [pred_class], seed_img)
    heatmaps.append(heatmap)

plt.imshow(utils.stitch_images(heatmaps))
plt.axis('off')
plt.title('Class Activation map')
plt.savefig('keras-vis-cam-2.jpeg')
```

issue_title: Derive developmental version build number from SCM through git describe
issue_body:
Originally discussed in #77.

It seems like bumpversion relies on information coded in files (`.bumpversion.cfg`, `setup.py`) and cannot read information provided by SCM.

When building PEP440 *developmental releases* (maybe in CI), the build number (how many commits since the last release) should be obtained from SCM (`git describe`) and and appended to version (e.g. `1.2.3.dev0`, `1.2.3.dev1`, ...).

Something to take a look at is https://pypi.python.org/pypi/setuptools-version-command, which already does this (in a bit different way).

issue_title: Upload results inaccurate on low-speed uplinks
issue_body:
I have a test setup with an uplink that is shaped to max 1Mbit/sec. Version 0.3.4 of speedtest-cli reliably reported upload speeds of ~0.83Mbit/s, but v1.0.0 reports upload speeds of ~1.69Mbit/s. The issue lies with the way speedtest-cli computes the upload speed, because the TX byte counters on the interface show results that are consistent with speedtest-cli 0.3.4.

I did some digging, and I think I have found a lead towards the source of the inaccuracy: the HTTPUploader class tallies bytes as they are passed on to urllib2 for uploading, but due to the slow uplink, all HTTPUploader instances raise SpeedtestTimeout before the full upload is complete.  My guess is that the final upload results therefore incorrectly counts a number of bytes as uploaded whereas they're really stuck in urllib's (or the outgoing interface's) TX buffer.

An extra data point that (I think) supports my assumption: if I increase the HTTPUploader timeout by a factor of 10, the uploads complete successfully (i.e. no SpeedTestTimeout exceptions are raised) and the reported upload speed is back down to ~0.9Mbit/s, which is in line with reality.

issue_title: "has_*side_castling_rights" behaves strange (in my eyes)
issue_body:
Hello.
Maybe i understand this 2 functions wrong.
But for me they should tell me future possibilities to castle.

If i use the NON-chess960 mode, i get wrong answers.
Seems the function only checks for the rook&king spaces in correct direction (that only helps me on chess960 mode).

Here is some code:
    fen_game = '1r2k3/8/8/8/8/8/8/R3KR2 w KQkq - 0 1'
    board = chess.Board(fen_game, False)
    print(board.has_queenside_castling_rights(chess.WHITE))
    print(board.has_queenside_castling_rights(chess.BLACK))
    print(board.has_kingside_castling_rights(chess.WHITE))
    print(board.has_kingside_castling_rights(chess.BLACK))
    print('')
    board = chess.Board(fen_game, True)
    print(board.has_queenside_castling_rights(chess.WHITE))
    print(board.has_queenside_castling_rights(chess.BLACK))
    print(board.has_kingside_castling_rights(chess.WHITE))
    print(board.has_kingside_castling_rights(chess.BLACK))

The first part should display: T,F,F,F but i get T,T,T,F - for example, as black i can never play 0-0-0 nor as white 0-0.
Similar problems with a wK on d1 for example.

issue_title: A possible bug in chess.svg.Arrow()
issue_body:
```
import chess
import chess.svg
import IPython

chessboard = chess.Board()
chessboardSVG = chess.svg.board(board=chessboard,
```

issue_title: How do I walk nodes that require forward searches
issue_body:
First off,  thanks for writing this! I've been avoiding formal parsing since lex and yacc were babes -- I always gritted my teeth and made do with regexes.   This is the first parser I've tried that really makes sense to me.  

Some years ago I developed  a little homemade musical notation language for rapidly writing down song melodies and fiddle tunes.  It borrows various ideas from ABC and Lilypond's input syntax but aims to be much simpler.  My innovation, if you can call it such, was realizing that since musicians think in beats it's possible to avoid having to explicitly specify note durations by grouping the pitch tokens together within beats and separating them with whitespace. For example,  a couple of bars from a jig in 6/8 time might look like `d-e f-g | agf edc | ...` It's quick to type or write by hand and readable for playing -- but not all that easy to parse with regexes.

I was really pleased when a couple of hours after downloading parsimonious I had the working grammar below.
```
import time
from parsimonious.grammar import Grammar
grammar = Grammar(
    """
    melody = bar+ ws*
    bar = (ws* beat ws)+ barline
    beat = subbeat+
    barline = "|"
    extendable = pitch /rest
    pitch = octave* alteration* pitchname
    subbeat = extendable / hold
    rest = "z"
    hold = "-"
    octave = octave_up / octave_down
    alteration = sharp / flat
    sharp = "#"
    flat = "&"
    octave_up = "^"
    octave_down = "/"
    pitchname = ~"[a-g]"i
    ws = " "*
    """
    )

if __name__ == '__main__':
    start = time.time()
    tree = grammar.parse(" c d#d | e zf | -g /g | c - | ")
    print(tree.prettily())
    print("Parsed in {} seconds".format(time.time() - start))
``` 

So now I'm trying to figure out how to use NodeVisitor to extract the info from the parse tree and create a stream of MIDI NoteOn/Noteoff events.  One of the bits I'm trying get my head around is handling the search ahead, search back operations implicit in a fragment like

```
a-b c-- | -de ...
```
Handling the 2nd beat of the first bar requires looking ahead to the first beat of the second bar to extend the duration of the `c` into the first 1/8th note past the downbeat.

Any help greatly appreciated!

Thanks,
Mike

issue_title: Possibility to make dataclasses iterable or indexable
issue_body:
Imagine users who are currently using named tuples or dictionaries as objects. They probably would want to switch to dataclasses when they appear, but probably in their code they have something like:
```python
name, age = person
# or
person['name'] = 'Eve'
```
I think it may easier for them to switch to dataclasses if we provide something like this:
```python
@data(iterable=True)
class Point:
    x: int
    y: int
origin = Point(0, 0)
x, y = origin  # OK

@data(indexable=True)
class Person:
    name: str
    age: int
person =  Person('John', 31)
name = person['name']
person['age'] = 32
```
I am not sure, but I think both flags should be ``False`` by default. If ``iterable`` is enabled, then we would add a corresponding ``__iter__``, for ``indexable`` we would add ``__getitem__`` and (depending on the mutability/hashability flags) ``__setitem__``.

issue_title: The serialization cliff
issue_body:
Seeing as the scope of dataclasses still seems fairly pliable, I'll share an answer to #19 ("why not attrs?") which could greatly increase its utility: The serialization cliff.

My teammates and I have been using attrs almost as long as it's been around, and namedtuples for much longer. Great and fine solutions, if your data doesn't have to leave the process space.

The boilerplate we're trying to avoid comes back in a severe way as soon as databases, SOA, or files get involved. Let alone anything with complicated validation rules.

I think to be true to the name, dataclasses need to account for the pervasiveness of data ingestion and emission. As it stands, attrs has `asdict()` and not much else. Nothing to help recursively (re)construct instances from data.

There is another, somewhat more popular, approach in [marshmallow](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow), which also aims to make data-centric, serialization-agnostic container types. It's not perfect, but it's a starting point. A balance of attrs and marshmallow features may yield a powerful and sufficiently-differentiated featureset that I think a lot of developers are missing in Python. The number of entries in this space seems to agree.

I'm happy to see more discussion going into these fundamentals, and I'm hopeful that more and more they take the whole data workflow into mind. :)

issue_title: Ambiguous return by authorize_response
issue_body:
Currently it seems that authorized_response can do one of three things:
1. Return a `None` value
2. Return an `OAuthException`
3. Return authorized response.

I am not quite sure about the motive for introducing 2 here: https://github.com/lepture/flask-oauthlib/commit/2d73e5edacd58e48a90dc0
Getting an exception object as response for an invalid grant is very surprising. Why not just propagate the exception?

issue_title: JSON Support
issue_body:
```python
def extract_params():
    """Extract request params."""

    uri = _get_uri_from_request(request)
    http_method = request.method
    headers = dict(request.headers)
    if 'wsgi.input' in headers:
        del headers['wsgi.input']
    if 'wsgi.errors' in headers:
        del headers['wsgi.errors']
    # Werkzeug, and subsequently Flask provide a safe Authorization header
    # parsing, so we just replace the Authorization header with the extraced
    # info if it was successfully parsed.
    if request.authorization:
        headers['Authorization'] = request.authorization

    body = request.form.to_dict()
    return uri, http_method, body, headers
````

extract_params uses `request.form` to get the body of the incoming request. If the incoming request's content-type is json, request.form will return an empty `ImmutableMultiDict`. Possibly use the [get_json](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/api/#flask.Request.get_json) method

issue_title: print something while pylint is running so CI services know it's still running
issue_body:
# Issue

CircleCI and probably other services timeout the test if no output is received. It would be helpful to print something while pylint is running to indicate the test hasn't stalled.

# One Idea

Print something during `handle_message`, for example:
```
    def handle_message(self, msg):
        """Get message and append to our data structure"""
        self.data.append(msg)
        print('.', end='')
        sys.stdout.flush()
```
And add another argument to enable this behavior. Downside is it just creates a lot of `.`. Don't know if there's something quick and more clever.

I'm happy to create a PR with this if you like.

Edit: A better option would probably be to override `on_set_current_module`
https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/blob/3a8635a9ef85759f96bfefd8edcd70e223fd12bb/pylint/reporters/__init__.py#L96
so we only have one dot per file.

issue_title: Crashing if metadata uses non UTF-8 characters
issue_body:
If an image file has metadata using non ASCII characters (e.g. accented letters) that are not UTF-8 encoded than Photini may crash when reading the metadata. The crash typically says something like "Error in `python3': munmap_chunk(): invalid pointer:" and prints a long backtrace.

As far as I can tell, this only happens when using Python3 and when using PyGobject rather than pgi. The quick fix is to install pgi: `sudo pip3 install pgi`.

As far as I can tell, this only happens when using Python3 and when using PyGobject rather than pgi. The quick fix is to install pgi: `sudo pip3 install pgi`.

issue_title: Deprecated Google Scopes
issue_body:
Hello, [the scopes used foor Google auth are deprecated](https://developers.google.com/+/web/api/rest/oauth). You need to replace it with a shorter alias.

|Deprecated|Current|
|:--|:--|
| `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/userinfo.profile` | `profile` |
| `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/userinfo.email` | `email` |

This is not a problem for the moment, I have spotted it while opening another issue for zestedesavoir/zds-site#2941.

By default, Windows disallows kerberos ticket export as the following registry key isn't set: 
```
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Kerberos\Parameters\allowtgtsessionkey
```
Without it set to 1, the `sessionkey` is null and the ticket isn't usable.
More info [here](http://support.microsoft.com/kb/308339) and [here](http://cert.europa.eu/static/WhitePapers/CERT-EU-SWP_14_07_PassTheGolden_Ticket_v1_3-fullversion.pdf).

Cheers.

issue_title: Access to context fails inside nested serializer
issue_body:
As discussed in #5 a DynamicFieldMixin-ed serializer that accesses `context` will fail when used as a nested serializer.

I found this issue when I was re-using a serializer as a nested serializer, and that serializer relies on `context['request']` to build a url for one of its fields.

Without fixing `drf-dynamic-fields` to allow nested serializers, there are a number of work arounds:

1. You could just have two separate serializers, one for nesting and one for normal views.
2. The nested serializer can manually find the correct `context` by chasing the `parent` fields up the tree until it finds the one with `context` set.

I used 2) for a while before deciding it was better to fix the underlying issue.

issue_title: CurrencyLayer API
Setting fields to be saved (fields option)
issue_body:
Hello,

I wonder if this setting (along with `exclude`) is working for someone. The problem is `VersionAdapter.get_fields_to_serialize` is a generator. Later this generator is used in `django.core.serializers.base.py`, `serialize` function to check if current field is in a list of fields to serialize. But generators are different than lists/tuples and operator `in` works differently:

```
>>> g = (x for x in range(1, 3)); 1 in g; 2 in g;
True
True

>>> g = (x for x in range(1, 3)); 1 in g; 1 in g;
True
False

>>> g = (x for x in range(1, 3)); 2 in g; 1 in g;
True
False

g = (x for x in range(2, 3)); 1 in g; 2 in g;
False
False
```

So, `VersionAdapter.get_fields_to_serialize` as generator will work only if it'll have all fields and in order `serialize` iterates them.

The solution could be simple: `VersionAdapter.get_serialized_data` function instead of using `get_fields_to_serialize` as generator will convert it to list:

```python
    def get_serialized_data(self, obj):
        """Returns a string of serialized data for the given obj."""
        return serializers.serialize(
            self.get_serialization_format(),
            (obj,),
            fields = list(self.get_fields_to_serialize()), # here we convert generator to a list
        )
```

Am I doing something wrong, or is it library bug?

* face_recognition version: latest/1.2.1
* Python version: 3.5.4/3.6.4
* Operating System: Windows/Ubuntu

(As the case came from different testers, I can't be more precise, but this seems platform independent)

### Description

Hi, we are discussing right now about an [issue about landmarks in deepfakes](https://github.com/deepfakes/faceswap/issues/187) (The link is SFW, but example faces clearly come from NSFW sources), happening on some faces detected with the `cnn` option. 

What we found is that, in the reported cases, `dlib.cnn_face_detection_model_v1` returns a very narrow sized face box and the landmarks returned by `dlib.shape_predictor` are totally wrong. It happens that if the same face is passed to the landmarks detector in a cropped image with more space around, the landmarks detection works fine.

Also we checked on `1adrianb/face-alignment` lib because it seems to have better results. It happens that [some preprocessing](https://github.com/1adrianb/face-alignment/blob/fecdd1084df5d52246d26a9eb210e3404b4f6345/face_alignment/api.py#L171) is done to add more space around the detected face before passing it to the landmarks detector (but maybe this is unrelated as it doesn't use dlib `shape_predictor`)

Also note that landmarks in `deepfakes` are returned by a [copy/paste of _raw_face_landmarks](https://github.com/deepfakes/faceswap/blob/51f1993d93e0ffb581d44416f327f0cf731c34e8/lib/faces_detect.py#L12) (as we needed a simple list of landmarks while `api.py` only offers `face_landmarks` that returns landmarks in a structured way). This shouldn't be the origin of the issue however.

Do you have any idea of the origin of the issue? Is that from a bad usage of lib? Would you recommend enlarging the face box returned by `dlib.cnn_face_detection_model_v1` to correct the problem?

Thanks and sorry if this is not relevant to your project

### What I Did

I ran the following code on images (`frame` variable) with faces from almost side view and quite rotated:
```
    face_locations = face_recognition.face_locations(frame, model=model)
    landmarks = _raw_face_landmarks(frame, face_locations)
```

issue_title: Memory leak with eventlet enabled
issue_body:
I'm experiencing memory leaks when using gunicorn eventlet workers.

Some digging shows me the leak comes from GreenThread objects not being released by RequestScope.
```python
def reset(self):
    self._local_manager.cleanup()
    self._locals.scope = {}
```
Here, the `self._local_manager.cleanup()`  pops the reference to the GreenThread object but `self._locals.scope = {}` adds the reference back to the `__storage__` dictionary held by `self._locals`, so GreenThread object is not dereferenced and causing the application memory usage increase.

The problem disappears when switching the order of the two lines. Any thought?

issue_title: Incompatible with multiple apps with different loader
issue_body:
`LoginManager` stores configs like `user_loader` in the manager object itself instead of `app.extensions['NAME']`.

This means that using the same `LoginManager` instance with multiple apps (each app running `login_manager.init_app(app)` won't work as expected: You can't use different `user_loader` for different apps.

Extensions like Flask-SQLAlchemy and Flask-Mail store their state in `app.extensions['NAME']`, so multiple apps can be configured separately.

issue_title: Unambiguous non-existent user/invalid password messages via JSON.
issue_body:
In addition to #357, Flask-Security gives attackers hints to whether the username or password is invalid. Even if you change `SECURITY_MSG_USER_DOES_NOT_EXIST` and `SECURITY_MSG_INVALID_PASSWORD` to use ambiguous wording, talking to the login view via JSON (for example) will still give hints whether the email or password is incorrect.

For an invalid password, the response looks like:
```
{'meta': {'code': 400},
 'response': {'errors': {'password': ['Invalid username or password']}}}
```

And a non existent user:
```
{'meta': {'code': 400},
 'response': {'errors': {'email': ['Invalid username or password']}}}
```

issue_title: absolute paths in css.map file
issue_body:
Hi, related to [64](https://github.com/jrief/django-sass-processor/issues/64) but only in parts. 

The problem is that `libsass` generates absolute paths in `css.map` file. I found that on Windows, but probably on *nix too, if `source_map_filename` parameter starts with root `/` slash  like `/static/base.css.map`  as it always the case because of this line: [L74](https://github.com/jrief/django-sass-processor/blob/43978f8c709d5f195229deb6ec7817a1815a4db6/sass_processor/processor.py#L74) `libsass` create absolute paths. If the `source_map_filename` starts without slash like `static/base.css.map` the generated paths would be relative as they should.

issue_title: Apache 2.4 EnableSendfile as a potential replacement for XSendfile
issue_body: Apache 2.4 is not supported officially by mod_xsendfile, but it seems the `EnableSendfile on` configuration option that ships with my Apache 2.4 installation accomplishes the same thing. Switching to `SENDFILE_BACKEND = 'sendfile.backends.xsendfile'` improves performance with this setting on regardless of whether or not mod_xsendfile is installed on the machine. I may have gotten lucky with my configuration, but I think `EnableSendfile on` should be investigated as the long-term replacement for mod_xsendfile for this package.

issue_title: HTML unescaping only replaces first ocurrance of HTML codes and does not handle & or "
issue_body: The helper method `_unescape_entities(xml_text)` does not handle HTML codes `&` or `"`. Unfortunately this precludes some useful template values such as `{{ date.strftime("%Y-%m-%d") }}`. In addition, any expression that involves more than one instance of escaped characters would also fail due to the regular expression substitution being performed only once.

issue_title: Documentation error SetReply::asset()
issue_body:
I stumbled upon this error in documentation, the set reply is said to have a method `asset()` that returns a set but it is implemented as a coroutine and therefore it returns a future of set and that means that the user of the API has to put `s = await reply.asset()` and not just `s = reply.asset()`. issue_title: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'user'
issue_body:
When I call `get_object` inside `get_serializer_context` swagger has no `request`. For example

```python
class SomeView(generics.CreateAPIView):

    def get_object(self):
        pk = self.kwargs.get('id')
        
        try:
            return Something.objects.get(
                pk=pk, owner=self.request.user  # Issue
            )
        except Something.DoesNotExist:
            raise exceptions.NotFound(_('Something does not exist.'))

    def get_serializer_context(self):
        context = super(SomeView, self).get_serializer_context()
        context['something'] = self.get_object()
        return context 
```
This works fine, except for swagger. Somehow it doesn't have request and it will throw 
```
AttributeError at /
'NoneType' object has no attribute 'user'

Request Method: GET
Request URL: http://localhost:8000/
Django Version: 1.10.1
Python Executable: ..../bin/python
Python Version: 2.7.10
```


If I try to go around this, and use
```python
user = self.request.user if request else None
```
it will display swagger page, but endpoint request (`?format=openapi`) will fail, throwing 'Something does not exist'

The only solution I have so far is using `get_queryset` instead of `get_object` and using first() in serializer, which makes more code than I would like.

Using
djangorestframework==3.5.4
django-rest-swagger==2.1.1

issue_title: non-inline-serialization
issue_body:
Hi,

I am attempting to serialize an object that has a `backref` to a list (potentially zero, one or many).

```python

class Person(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'person'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = Column(String(128))
    surname = Column(String(128))


class Score(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'score'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    person_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('person.id'))
    person = relationship('Person', backref='scores')
    value = Column(Integer)
```

This will serialize `Person` like so:

```
{'id': '1',
 'name': u'Jayne',
 'scores': [{'id': '1', 'person_id': '1', 'value': '1000'},
            {'id': '2', 'person_id': '1', 'value': '2000'}],
 'surname': u'Cobb'}
```

I've tried to find a way to change this serialization to something like this:

```
{'id': '1',
 'name': u'Jayne',
 'scores': ['1', '2'],
 'surname': u'Cobb'}
```

I don't seem to be able to. It doesn't seem to be possible to change the `typ` of a relationship with the `info` dictionary.

Obviously it is possible to add this:

```python
    __colanderalchemy_config__ = {
        'excludes': ['person_id', 'person', 'value']
    }
```

 to reduce the serialization of person to this:

````
{'id': '1',
 'name': u'Jayne',
 'scores': [{'id': '1'}, {'id': '2'}],
 'surname': u'Cobb'}
```

which is a bit better, but not great. However, when I then want to serialize score specifically, it does not include those excluded fields either.

I have create a working example in this gist: https://gist.github.com/dpwrussell/7a72fee067a40f4dca97

Is this impossible?

Cheers

issue_title: Add support for string as model name in G, N
issue_body:
I'm using this mockey patched version of `G` that accepts `app_name.ClassName` string format similar to how you would use in `models.ForeignKey()`.

```python
from django_dynamic_fixture import G as __G

def G(model, **kwargs):
    """Overrides the G to be able to use it with 'app_name.model_name'
    format as well.

    >>> G('users.User', email="mail@examle.com")
    <User: mail@examle.com>
    >>> G(User, email="mail@example.com")
    <User: mail@examle.com>
    """
    if isinstance(model, str):
        from django.apps import apps
        model = apps.get_model(model)
    return __G(model, **kwargs)
```

I think it would be a nice add-on to the library itself. @paulocheque thoughts, I'll submit a PR.

issue_title: Keep Robot log files small
issue_body:
There should be Keyword to set a global loglevel instead of at the individual keyword level on error (Page Should Contain Element etc). Also all the Wait keywords don't even have a loglevel parameter and automatically log the source...

`
 def _wait_until_no_error(self, timeout, wait_func, *args):
        timeout = robot.utils.timestr_to_secs(timeout) if timeout is not None else self._timeout_in_secs
        maxtime = time.time() + timeout
        while True:
            timeout_error = wait_func(*args)
            if not timeout_error:
                return
            if time.time() > maxtime:
                **self.log_source()**
                raise AssertionError(timeout_error)
            time.sleep(0.2)

`

There are many instance, for example scrolling, where there is a need to run and ignore errors. When this happens the logs get very large as it logs the source every time.

``

### Code
``js
# web3 == 4.0.0b3
import web3
from web3 import Web3, HTTPProvider
from web3.providers.eth_tester import EthereumTesterProvider
from eth_tester import EthereumTester
from solc import compile_source

# Solidity source code
contract_source_code = '''
pragma solidity ^0.4.18;

contract C {
    event Test(uint indexed x);
    function test()  {
        Test(2);
    }
}
'''

compiled_sol = compile_source(contract_source_code) # Compiled source code
contract_interface = compiled_sol['<stdin>:C']
print (contract_interface['abi'])

# web3.py instance
eth_tester = EthereumTester()
web3 = Web3(EthereumTesterProvider(eth_tester))
accounts = eth_tester.get_accounts()
# web3 = Web3(HTTPProvider("http://localhost:8545"))
# accounts = web3.eth.accounts

# Instantiate and deploy contract
contract = web3.eth.contract(abi=contract_interface['abi'], bytecode=contract_interface['bin'])

# Get transaction hash from deployed contract
tx_hash = contract.deploy(transaction={'from': accounts[0], 'gas': 410000})

# Get tx receipt to get contract address
tx_receipt = web3.eth.getTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
contract_address = tx_receipt['contractAddress']

# Contract instance 
contract_instance = web3.eth.contract(address=contract_address, abi=contract_interface['abi'])

transfer_filter = contract_instance.eventFilter('Test')

# Should be empty
print (transfer_filter.get_all_entries())
    
# Call function
tx_hash = contract_instance.transact({'from': accounts[0]}).test();
# Receipt's logs must have the event
receipt = web3.eth.getTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
print (receipt)

# Should print the event
print (transfer_filter.get_new_entries())
``


### How can it be fixed?

Could be fixed by decoding the transaction properly, haven't thought of the implementation though.

issue_title: Timeout Sending 50Transactions parallel to private network node
issue_body:
* Version: geth 1.8.11, go 1.10.2
* Python: 3.6
* OS: win


Due to some Latency-Measurements i send 50 Txs via python multiprocessing simultaneously to one of my three nodes, which are my private network.
After i get some results some error occurs and i don't know how to fix this timeout issue.

My Pythoncode:

```
import multiprocessing as mp
import time
import os
import socket
socket.timeout(100000)
from web3 import Web3
web3 = Web3(Web3.HTTPProvider("http://127.0.0.1:8545", request_kwargs={'timeout': 60}))

#Get Access to Coinbase-Account
web3.personal.unlockAccount(web3.eth.coinbase, 'xxxx')

#Testsetgröße
n = 50
output = mp.Queue()

#Function to transfer Token and measure Latency for each
def measure_Latency(output):
    tStart = time.time()
    hTx = web3.eth.sendTransaction({
        'from': web3.toChecksumAddress(web3.eth.coinbase),
        'to': web3.toChecksumAddress('0x4e3913e7fdaa548fbe60ebf330169ccc576ba1df'),
        'value': web3.toWei(1, 'ether')
        })
    web3.eth.waitForTransactionReceipt(web3.toHex(hTx))
    tEnd = time.time()
    tResult = tEnd - tStart
    output.put(tResult)
    print("Latenz:",tResult, "Sekunden")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    for x in range(n):
        #init each transactionprocess
        MeasureInstances= mp.Process(target=measure_Latency, args=(output,))
        MeasureInstances.start()
    for x in range(n):
        #wait until each transaction is done
        MeasureInstances.join()
    results = [output.get() for x in range(n)]
    #write results in .txt-file
    filename = 'Latency_Testset'+str(n)+'_'+str(time.strftime("%d-%m-%Y_%H-%M-%S"))+'.txt'
    pathname = os.path.join('results', filename)
    file = open(pathname,'w')
    for x in range(len(results)):
        file.write('{:.9f}'.format(results[x]) + '\n')
    file.close()
```

issue_title: Where did the ENS API docs go?
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

The low-level API docs for the `ens` module are gone... See https://web3py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ens.html


### How can it be fixed?

:man_shrugging: Make sure all docs warnings are resolved, and futz with the docs settings.

issue_title: TuRF doesn't work with odd number of features
issue_body:
When the number of features is odd, TuRF often leaves out one feature (causing a value error at this line https://github.com/EpistasisLab/scikit-rebate/blob/master/skrebate/turf.py#L166) because segmenting of features into selected and non_selected is based on the number of features to retain:
https://github.com/EpistasisLab/scikit-rebate/blob/master/skrebate/turf.py#L131

This code fixes the problem for me:
```
num_features_non_select = len(features_iter[iter_count]) - num_features
non_select = np.array(features_iter[iter_count].argsort()[:num_features_non_select])
```

issue_title: No license?
issue_body:
Could you please add a clear indication of what the license of this code is? Without this, it will be impossible to use for companies and free software projects alike. Simply add a section to the `README`, and/or add a `LICENSE` file containing the full license. If you want to be paranoid, add a reference to the license file, or a shortened version of the license to the source code as well.

If I may make a suggestion: a MIT/BSD license would be liberal enough to ensure it can be most widely used.

issue_title: Use gulp to build JS and CSS
issue_body:
We previously used django-compressor to compile LESS to CSS and also to minify our JS. Django-compressor was removed in #1568. Currently we don't process JS in any way and use a simple Makefile for compiling LESS. The objective is make our CSS/JS process more powerful, while keeping it completely optional if you don't want to touch the JS/CSS.

Requirements:
* no new runtime requirements - this means, all generated JS/CSS files must be committed to the repository in the correct locations
* minified versions for production deployment
* sourcemap support for development

issue_title: Enable @hook and @when (et al.) to be used together
issue_body:
For consistent and correct ordering of handlers, there is a "hook phase" in which all `@hook` handlers are triggered, _before_ any `@when` handlers are triggered.  However, this leads to the surprising result that you cannot combine `@hook` with any other decorator, even though it is often useful and intuitive to do so.

If possible, we should support this use-case, even if it can sometimes lead to odd corner-case behavior, as long as the behavior is consistent and well-documented.

issue_title: Warning on the catching of all exception types
issue_body:
*Original report by [conrad-p-dean](https://launchpad.net/~conrad-p-dean) on [Launchpad](https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/1628263):*

------------------------------------

It's generally bad practice to catch all exception types like this:

```python
   try:
      o = open('file.txt', 'rb')
      data = json.loads(o.read())
   except:
      print("Can't parse file")
```

For example, sometimes the `except` block handles the wrong sort of exception.  There are many sorts of exceptions that could have been thrown in the example above (file not found, bad json format, etc), and the original exception would have done a much better job of informing us of what went wrong. The author should only catch specific types of exceptions for specific ways to handle them.

It would be great if a rule could be added to catch instances where all exceptions are being handled, either in the above form or with the catch-all `except Exception:`. Where an explicit catch-all could be silenced with a `# NOQA` in instances where they really want everything caught.

issue_title: integration tests should not delete failed stacks
issue_body: We should destroy these stacks' resources but should leave the stacks themselves around for easier investigation. We can use a simple Pulumi bot to clean up old stacks periodically.

issue_title: Make the local backend a little more prominent
issue_body:
The CLI actively pushes folks towards using the service for state management. We do have a local backend, but the only documentation on it is in our FAQ: https://pulumi.io/reference/faq.html#can-i-use-pulumi-without-depending-on-pulumicom

We'd like to make a little easier to discover and use the local state management stuff. To that end, we're going to make a set of targeted improvements:

1. Simplify the gesture to use the local backend to just `pulumi login --local` instead of `pulumi login --cloud-url local://` (the former becomes an alias for the latter).
2. When we prompt for login, add a note explaining that you can `CTRL+C` and run pulumi login `--local` to use the local back-end.
3. When using the local back-end, explicitly show the location of the checkpoint file instead of a link to the service for update history.
4. Fix the cases where we don't prompt to confirm some operations or run previews when using the local backend.

Longer team, we may try to revisit our previous approach of being able to manage local stacks and remote stacks concurrently (e.g. support being logged into multiple back-ends concurrently) but for now we are okay with the bimodal experience of "you're either logged into the local system or pulumi.com"

issue_title: Confidant strips newlines when submitting credentials through webpage
issue_body:
We're using Confidant to store, among other things, our certificates. When making requests between our machines we noticed that they were failing due to a SSL certificate verification error. Through some investigation, I found that the md5sum of the certificate is different after it's been sent through Confidant. 

It seems like this is caused by the angular.js frontend, which I believe is stripping off newlines. I tried pasting in a value with a newline at the end, confirmed that the blank line was there after pasting, and found it to be missing when editing the credential again after saving.

I then tried to edit the value by adding newlines and noticed that the textarea class is still marked as "ng-pristine".
````
