issue_title: Pipenv should check implementation version when choosing a runtime
issue_body:
##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type: macOS 10.13.3
1. Python version: `$ python -V`
```
Python 2.7.13 (c925e7381036, Jun 05 2017, 20:53:58)
[PyPy 5.8.0 with GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 5.1 (clang-503.0.40)]
```
1. Pipenv version: `$ pipenv --version`: pipenv, version 9.1.0

##### Expected result

```
$ pyenv local pypy2.7-5.8.0
# Creates .python-version that sets the project's python 
# version to the specific pypy version
$ pipenv install --python pypy
# Installs packages in virtualenv created with pypy2.7-5.8.0
```

##### Actual result

```
$ pipenv install --python pypy
Creating a virtualenv for this project…
Using /Users/josh/.pyenv/versions/pypy2.7-5.9.0/bin/pypy to create virtualenv…
# Installs packages in virtualenv created with pypy2.7-5.9.0, 
# or whatever the latest version of pypy2.7 I have installed is
```

I have to uninstall pypy2.7-5.9.0 and any other later versions to be able to use v5.8.0 with pipenv. I need to test with 5.8.0 specifically because that is the latest version that the heroku python buildpack supports.

Perhaps pipenv should defer to `.python-version` when choosing the python version to use, unless it conflicts with `python_version` or `python_full_version` in the `Pipfile`, in which case throw an error.

issue_title: Installing package from different sources depending on sys_platform
issue_body:
I am trying to achieve the following in a Pipefile. Notice the double bsddb3:

```
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[packages]
bsddb3 = {version = "==6.2.6", sys_platform = "!= 'win32'"}
bsddb3 = {file = "https://download.lfd.uci.edu/pythonlibs/l8ulg3xw/bsddb3-6.2.6-cp36-cp36m-win_amd64.whl", sys_platform = "== 'win32'", platform_machine = "== 'AMD64'"}

[requires]
python_version = "3.6"

```

The problem with this is that I get an exception saying _Duplicate keys found_

bsddb3 requires installation like the above for the Windows platform. But for my Linux users they should still just install the normal bsddb3. How would I achieve this, as the above clearly does not work?

issue_title: Installing tornado from Pipfile{,.lock} losts singledispatch dependency in Python 2.7
issue_body:
##### Issue description

In Python 2.7, installing from Pipfile{,lock} containing `tornado` does not install its `singledispatch` dependency.

##### Expected result

`singledispatch` is installed.

##### Actual result

`singledispatch` is not installed and `tornado` is unusable.

##### Steps to replicate

1. Create a `Pipfile` according to detailed info below.
2. Run `pipenv install --two`
3. ```

       from tornado import gen
     File "/home/thc/.local/share/virtualenvs/test-50RnJwkM/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tornado/gen.py", line 112, in <module>
       from singledispatch import singledispatch  # backport
   ImportError: No module named singledispatch
   >>>

issue_title: Generated requirements.txt complains about unpinned versions
issue_body:
Pipenv is creating `requirements.txt` files that fail installation complaining about unpinned versions in hashes mode.  This may be related to #491 or #357, but as those are closed and this is reproduceable in 9.0.3, I thought I should post it.

##### Describe your environment

1. Arch Linux
1. Python version: 3.6.4
1. Pipenv version: 9.0.3

##### Expected result

Unless I'm not understanding the feature correctly, one should be able to setup a virtualenv and use the generated `requirements.txt` file to populate it without need of a Pipfile or pipenv, but the file being generated isn't working.

issue_title: sdist race condition and temp dir customization
issue_body:
Currently if two sdist commands are ran at the same time there is a race condition. One sdist command can be purging the temp files under {packagename}-{packageversion} in the setup.py directory while the other is zipping it, causing error messages like this

```
Creating tar archive
removing 'cosmo4d-0.0.0' (and everything under it)
error removing cosmo4d-0.0.0: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'cosmo4d-0.0.0/setup.py'
error removing cosmo4d-0.0.0: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'cosmo4d-0.0.0/cosmo4d/tests/test_engine.py'
error removing cosmo4d-0.0.0: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'cosmo4d-0.0.0/cosmo4d/tests/test_pmesh.py'
error removing cosmo4d-0.0.0: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'cosmo4d-0.0.0/cosmo4d/tests/test_map2d.py'
error removing cosmo4d-0.0.0: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'cosmo4d-0.0.0/cosmo4d/tests'
error removing cosmo4d-0.0.0: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'cosmo4d-0.0.0/cosmo4d/iotools.py'
error removing cosmo4d-0.0.0: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'cosmo4d-0.0.0/cosmo4d/pmeshengine.py'
error removing cosmo4d-0.0.0: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'cosmo4d-0.0.0/cosmo4d/__init__.py'
error removing cosmo4d-0.0.0: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'cosmo4d-0.0.0/cosmo4d/version.py'
error removing cosmo4d-0.0.0: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'cosmo4d-0.0.0/cosmo4d/options.py'
Processing /tmp/VlnJRkP/cosmo4d-0.0.0.tar.gz
Skipping bdist_wheel for cosmo4d, due to binaries being disabled for it.
```

One possible solution to avoid the racing is to allow an argument --temproot to allow redirection of the temp directory to a different location. Then the caller to setup.py (or sdist itself) can use `mktemp -d` to avoid building sdist from the same temp dir; or at least to minimize the chances of racing.

issue_title: Drop support for Python 2.7
issue_body:
It's possible we already have a ticket for this, but #1457 revived the idea. Here are my thoughts:

I'm interested in dropping support for new releases on Python 2.7. I can imagine maintaining support for Python 2.7 in a bugfix-only branch. My main worry would be breaking the expectation that the latest version of setuptools is generally viable in all supported versions. But there are features now that would mitigate that issue -- the python_version declaration and PEP 518 support in pip, I'm thinking systems admins and app developers would still be able to install the "latest" version of setuptools, even in Python 2.7, but they would only get the maintenance versions. Presumably the same thing happened for Python 2.6. I'd really like to drop support for Python 2.7 earlier than later if possible.

issue_title: raise connection errors from BrokerConnection.connect()
issue_body:
[This change](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/pull/564) may have been ill-conceived, as pointed out in #634 by @smueller18. `BrokerConnection.connect()` failing to connect to a broker and returning without any indication of that error makes no sense to me - a proper interface for this function would have it raising the appropriate exception upon failure to connect. `BrokerConnection.connect()` and `Broker.connect()` should both be changed to raise connection exceptions to their callers, and the callers should be adjusted to expect these exceptions such that issues like #543 don't reoccur.

**PyKafka version**: https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/commit/8f4617c42a3508d97d02e05a590e33482bf89b9a

issue_title: reset_offset_on_start edge case
issue_body:
The situation:

* Run a `BalancedConsumer` with `reset_offset_on_start=True` and `auto_commit_enable=True`.
* Observe that it starts at the latest available offset.
* Before the first autocommit, start another `BalancedConsumer` with the same settings.
* Observe that the first consumer consumes the entire history of the topic.

This is an error. It happens because `_reset_offsets` is not called on rebalances. Since the rebalance happens before an autocommit has occurred, the initial `fetch_offsets` call after the rebalance finds nothing and defaults to `0`. `_reset_offsets` is not called on rebalances since it can result in dropped messages: any messages produced during the rebalance will be skipped when the newly created internal consumer goes straight to `OFFSET_LATEST`.

How should this be handled? The current workaround is to set `auto_commit_interval` to a short enough timespan that this situation is very unlikely to happen, but it feels like we should have a smarter way of dealing with this.

Input from @kbourgoin welcome.

issue_title: Spout prematurely acks, even failed Bolt tuples
issue_body:
Hello,

I've had problems calling a Spouts `fail()` method in the boilerplate wordcount project. According to the pystorm [quickstart docs](https://github.com/pystorm/pystorm/blob/master/doc/source/quickstart.rst#failed-tuples) and numerous things I've read, calling `fail(tuple)` in a Bolt should elicit a failure in the originating Spout. However, even with the few modifications I've made, I always get a Spout `ack()` right when it leaves the Spout. Is this the correct behavior, or do I need to change a setting/instance variable?

I'm on `streamparse 3.4.0` and `storm 1.0.2`. 

My logs show the Spout `ack()` coming before the Bolt logging.

```
7609 [Thread-14-word_spout-executor[2 2]] INFO  o.a.s.s.ShellSpout - ShellLog pid:35761, name:word_spout 2017-02-20 15:30:33,070 - pystorm.component.word_spout - acking w
hen I shouldnt tup_id: 3219

...

7611 [Thread-21] INFO  o.a.s.t.ShellBolt - ShellLog pid:35760, name:count_bolt 2017-02-20 15:30:33,072 - pystorm.component.count_bolt - BOLT: receiving tup_id/count: 3219

```

`word.py`
```
from itertools import cycle

from streamparse import Spout
from streamparse import ReliableSpout


class WordSpout(ReliableSpout):
    outputs = ['word']

    def initialize(self, stormconf, context):
        self.words = cycle(['dog', 'cat', 'zebra', 'elephant'])
        self.count = 0

    def next_tuple(self):
        self.count += 1
        word = next(self.words)
        self.emit([str(self.count)], tup_id=self.count)

    def ack(self, tup_id):
        self.logger.info("acking when I shouldnt tup_id: {0}".format(tup_id))

    def fail(self, tup_id):
        self.logger.info("failing when I should tup_id: {0}".format(tup_id))

```

`wordcount.py`

```
import os
from collections import Counter

from streamparse import Bolt


class WordCountBolt(Bolt):
    auto_ack = False
    auto_fail = False

    outputs = ['word', 'count']

    def initialize(self, conf, ctx):
        self.counter = Counter()
        self.pid = os.getpid()
        self.total = 0

    def _increment(self, word, inc_by):
        self.counter[word] += inc_by
        self.total += inc_by

    def process(self, tup):
        word = tup.values[0]
        self._increment(word, 10 if word == "dog" else 1)
        # if self.total % 1000 == 0:
            # self.logger.info("counted [{:,}] words [pid={}]".format(self.total,
                                                                    # self.pid))
        self.logger.info("BOLT: receiving tup_id/count: {0}".format(word))
        # self.emit([word, self.counter[word]])
        self.fail(tup)
```
To reiterate, from what I know, I would expect the Bolt's failing to elicit a fail in the Spout and I would also not expect an ack so early. Am I wrong in thinking?

-jgujgu

issue_title: Incorrect nimbus value results in sparse submit exit without error message.
issue_body:
`sparse submit` exits without error message, only usage, if nimbus is set incorrectly to a non-empty value in config.json.

If I run the wordcount quickstart:

```
sparse quickstart wordcount
cd wordcount
sparse submit

              {jar,kill,list,quickstart,remove_logs,run,stats,submit,tail,update_virtualenv,visualize,worker_uptime}

[<Field: name (str, unique)>, <Field: description (str, not required)>, *** AttributeError: 'function' object has no attribute 'replace'
```

This appears to be due to the fact that [our custom types](https://github.com/ansible/tower-cli/blob/master/lib/tower_cli/utils/types.py) don't have [a `__name__` attribute](https://github.com/ansible/tower-cli/blob/master/lib/tower_cli/models/fields.py#L71). We should to go through all our custom types and assure that `__name__` is there and that the `__repr__` function works.

It looks like our inherited `ParamType`s from `click` also don't have this stuff, so there may be some potential for contributing upstream.

issue_title: Fully connected layer weight initialization
issue_body:
While using bias initialization with fully connected layer, there is an error while using 

```
initial = np.array([[1., 0, 0], [0, 1., 0]])
initial = initial.astype('float32')
initial = initial.flatten()
b_fc_loc2 = tf.Variable(initial_value=initial, name='b_fc_loc2')

loc_network= fully_connected(loc_network, 6, bias_init=b_fc_loc2 , activation='tanh')

```
you will get an error, 
`ValueError: If initializer is a constant, do not specify shape.
`
Solution: in the fully_connected function, use 
```
if bias:
    if isinstance(bias_init, str):
        bias_init = initializations.get(bias_init)()
        b = va.variable('b', shape=[n_units], initializer=bias_init,
                        trainable=trainable, restore=restore)
    
    else:
        b = va.variable('b', initializer=bias_init,
                        trainable=trainable, restore=restore)
    tf.add_to_collection(tf.GraphKeys.LAYER_VARIABLES + '/' + name, b)

```

issue_title: Support default t build_type
issue_body:
Hi!

To save CI time, I have avoided to build Debug packages. They are rarely used and spend precious time in our CI (travis, appveyor, gitlab). To avoid such build, I have used:

```python
from conan.packager import ConanMultiPackager

if __name__ == "__main__":
    builder = ConanMultiPackager()
    builder.add_common_builds(shared_option="foo:shared")
    filtered_builds = []
    for settings, options, env_vars, build_requires in builder.builds:
        if settings["build_type"] == "Release":
             filtered_builds.append([settings, options, env_vars, build_requires])
    builder.builds = filtered_builds
    builder.run()
```

or 

```python
from conan.packager import ConanMultiPackager

if __name__ == "__main__":
    builder = ConanMultiPackager(args="-s build_type=Release")
    builder.add_common_builds(shared_option="foo:shared")
    builder.run()
```

Would be great if [ConanMultiPackager](https://github.com/conan-io/conan-package-tools/blob/develop/conan/packager.py#L70) support an argument to choice only a build type:

```python
from conan.packager import ConanMultiPackager

if __name__ == "__main__":
    builder = ConanMultiPackager(build_type="Release")
    builder.add_common_builds(shared_option="foo:shared")
    builder.run()
```

Also, such feature could be used as environment variable, as `CONAN_BUILD_TYPE`, accepting only one  type. e.g. `CONAN_BUILD_TYPE=Release`

Regards.

issue_title: Consider switching from bleach to html-sanitizer
issue_body:
`bleach`'s dependency on `html5lib` has exhibited some pip-related issues: #588
And `bleach` is also hard to upgrade: #427

`html-sanitizer` seems to depend on `lxml` and `beautifulsoup4`. Using `html-sanitizer` might do away with those issues. Functionally, `html-sanitizer` also lets us filter for tags and attributes.
https://pypi.python.org/pypi/html-sanitizer/

issue_title: Removing name scope bug
issue_body:
just take this [mnist](https://github.com/tensorflow/models/blob/master/tutorials/image/cifar10/cifar10_multi_gpu_train.py#L92) for example:

in fact, under the `name_scope` context, the explicitly specify the summary name will still equipped with the `name_scope` prefix, i.e,  the so called `Remove 'tower_[0-9]/' from the name` doesn't work actually.

Also pointed at  [tf summary name not work](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/51382616/tf-summary-name-not-work)  and [removeing name scope](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/43814466/tensorflow-removing-name-scope-when-plotting-summary)

issue_title: Made a compatible router that works with the new python types

issue_title: Bucket name used in functional test should have a prefix

issue_title: Improved Kernel Status
issue_body:
We need to really double down on how we view the current kernel status:

* If we haven't gotten a message back from the kernel in a long time, we should ask for status and declare it as disconnected in the UI
* Any kernel process logs should end up in a "log drawer", similar to play
* If the internet goes out (within any of the web apps), we should evaluate if the kernel is still connected and display something about being "offline"

issue_title: Percentiles value is lost during computing group_sequential_delta
issue_body:
The parameter ```percentiles``` is lost during the following stack of calls:
-> ```experiment.group_sequential_delta``` (has input para percentiles)
-> ```early_stopping.group_sequential``` (lost input para percentiles)
-> ```statistics.normal_difference``` (has para percentiles again, but now we can only use the default value since the percentiles value user passed will be lost in previous step)

issue_title: python3.7: "async" is a keyword now
issue_body:
Installing patroni with python3.7 fails: (https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=904369)
```
patroni (1.4.4-2) wird eingerichtet ...
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/patroni/ha.py", line 610
    'offline':          dict(stop='fast', checkpoint=False, release=False, offline=True, async=False),
                                                                                             ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax
```
The problem exists also in python3-kubernetes where the parameter was renamed from "async" to "async_req" in 7.0.0a1: https://github.com/kubernetes-client/python/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md

A new release with a fix would be appreciated so we can update the Debian package. Thanks!

issue_title: sysid is not rechecked when coming out of pause
issue_body: `Postgresql.sysid` is cached and the cache is only invalidated by Patroni creating a new replica. If somebody puts the cluster in pause mode, stops postgres, overwrites data dir with a wrong backup by accident, restarts postgres and resumes Patroni will not notice a problem and will happily continue until restarted.

issue_title: PostgreSQL taking a long time to start up is considered running and healthy
issue_body:
When PostgreSQL is taking a long time to start up either due to crash recovery or WAL needed to reach consistent state not being available pg_ctl -w will timeout and return with error code 0.  Postgresql.start() will happily consider such server to be running. This is, at best, misleading.

There seems to be at least one race condition here. 

1. Master boots up and starts Patroni
2. In first iteration of run_cycle Ha.recover() is called, this asynchronously starts postgres.
3. Lock ttl expires.
4. The master is now starting up, will not participate in the race due to async action.
5. Standby will consider itself healthiest because master is not reachable (xlog key missing from JSON) acquires leader key, promotes itself.
6. pg_ctl start timeout expires on old master, it sets role to be replica in Postgresql._do_follow need_rewind == False branch.
7. run_cycle calls follow to restart master with new primary_conninfo while in the middle of recovery, because role != master it will not get rewound.

Basically 3 possible outcomes after a crash

1. Master manages to reacquire the lock in handle_long_action_in_progress in second iteration of run_cycle, will continue to be master once recovery completes. Although it is marked as running when pg_ctl timeout expires, despite not yet accepting connections. Somewhat confusing, but does not require administrator intervention.
2. Lock times out after first but before second iteration, leading to rewind not getting called.
3. Lock times out before first iteration, rewind will get called, everything is peachy.

In some cases (huge checkpoint_timeout, availability is priority over durability) it might be preferrable to refrain from acquiring leader lock before crash recovery is completed.

issue_title: Unable to install connexion in environments without access to global pypi on python2.X.  Please, consider building py2 wheels
issue_body:
### Description
Due to current pip/setuptools behavior packages listed in setup_requires are always downloaded from global pypi, regardless of index argument passed to pip install (please, see pypa/setuptools#209) for more details. This makes it impossible to install source distribution in environments without access to global pypi on python2.7.

### Expected behaviour

Should be able to install connexion in environments that can't access global pypi on python2.7.X

### Actual behaviour

fails to install in python2.7.X because it can't reach global pypi to get flake8

### Steps to reproduce

* Download all dependencies locally (in /tmp/connexion/depts, for example)
* Disconnect from internet.  If you have a network without access to global pypi, that'll work too
* create py2.7 venv and `pip install connexion --find-packages=file:///tpm/connexion/depts -v`

The command will fail unable to find flake8
### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

- `python --version`
2.7.12
- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`
any version

### Possible solutions
* add setup.cfg to the project with the following content:
```
[bdist_wheel]
universal=1
```
this will allow release.sh to build py2py3 wheels which don't suffer from setuptools bug mentioned above

* figure which setup.py commands need ['flake8'] and do something along the lines of
```
import sys
...
need = {'flake8'}.intersection(sys.argv)
setup_requires = ['flake8'] if need else []
```

issue_title: Backwards incompatible change for migrations?
issue_body:
Just upgraded a project from Django 1.7 to 1.8 and installed 9cc56318b. When I attempted to create a new migration, I got the following error:

    django.core.exceptions.ImproperlyConfigured: SalesforceAutoField must be an auto created primary key with name 'id' (as configured by settings).

This error was not clear to me and didn't suggest any specific fix since as far as I know, the pk's *are* named 'id'. The only way I was able to resolve this error was to delete the existing initial migration and recreate it. The only significant change I could detect was going from:

    ('id', salesforce.fields.SalesforceAutoField(default=None, primary_key=True, db_column='Id', serialize=False)),

to:

    ('id', salesforce.fields.SalesforceAutoField(auto_created=True, verbose_name='ID', db_column='Id', primary_key=True, serialize=False)),

Once I edited the existing migration to change this line for each model (all created via inspectdb), the problem went away.

Was there some setting I was supposed to change along the way? Or was it expected that migrations created in 1.7 would need to be edited in place for 1.8? Either way, some note in the README would be helpful and appreciated. Thank you @philchristensen and @hynekcer for your work on this library, it has been very useful!

issue_title: list-kernel performance issue
issue_body:
Right now after the SoS Jupyter frontend starts, it sends an request to the kernel for a list of kernels using code

```
events.on('kernel_ready.Kernel', request_kernel_list); 
```

The kernel then compiles a list of available kernels, basically running `jupyter kernelspec list` and send the frontend the list. However, this has caused `TravisCI` to [fail](https://travis-ci.org/vatlab/SOS/builds/220428500), and [removing this function](https://github.com/vatlab/SOS/commit/585610add180cbfa90b6bc07089c42b8697835f6) has [fixed the issue](https://travis-ci.org/vatlab/SOS/builds/220530360).

The question is then how to send the kernel list information efficiently to the frontend.

issue_title: Global `task` folder
issue_body: Sorry for submitting many tickets but this one certainly is of low priority (tagged to `later`). The number of `task` files accumulated in `~/.sos` is a bit worrisome to me. I'm wondering how many files do people usually have there? I removed my `~/.sos/task` on Friday morning, and by this time I've got 61072 files in `~/.sos/task`. This is two days working on just one project. I understand the advantages to have global task but I'm worried there will eventually be too many files there. I'm thinking maybe we have to move it to local `.sos`, or an option for it at least; or some way to clean it up when there are too many of them ...

issue_title: sos preparation changes status of remote task.
issue_body:
Merging result file to task file has some bad consequences. For example,

1. `sos run task -q remote`. The `task` file is transmitted to remote host and completed.
2. `sos run task -q remote` again. The `task` file is transmitted again to remote host, overwriting existing task file, and mark its status as `new`.

With the old implementation, copying `.task` file over will not change the status of remote task, and the task will not be rerun. This is no longer the case now.

The solution seems to stop touch remote `task` file during task preparation (which calls `rcp` to send file over),

issue_title: Unicode tokens cause IndexError
issue_body:
When trying to tag an address that has unicode tokens (after tokenization) the resulting clean token in tokenFeatures is an empty string causing an IndexError.
```
>>> import usaddress
>>> usaddress.tag('1 å drive')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/usaddress/__init__.py", line 84, in tag
    for token, label in parse(address_string) :
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/usaddress/__init__.py", line 73, in parse
    features = tokens2features(tokens)
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/usaddress/__init__.py", line 168, in tokens2features
    token_features = tokenFeatures(token)
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/usaddress/__init__.py", line 141, in tokenFeatures
    features = {'abbrev' : token_clean[-1] == u'.',
IndexError: string index out of range
```

The fix (I think) should be:
```
token_clean = re.sub(r'(^[\W]*)|([^.\w]*$)', u'', token)
```
to
```
 token_clean = re.sub(r'(^[\W]*)|([^.\w]*$)', u'', token, flags=re.UNICODE)
```

issue_title: Co-occurrence matrix only works for 8-bit images.
issue_body:
## Description
The co-occurrence matrix implemented in feature/texture.py (skimage.feature.greycomatrix) does not accept images with more than 256 levels. This is reasonable for the calculation of Haralick features, but I think that it might be useful to remove this limitation for other use cases. 

As an example, I am currently working on an application, where I want to build a neighboring graph of several objects in an image. For this, I calculate a Voronoï tessellation (objects are the seeds), which partitions the image plane into labeled regions. Finding touching regions corresponds to identifying pairs of values with co-occurrence > 0. The following code does that: 

`        
cooc = skimage.feature.greycomatrix(labels, [1], 
                                            [0, np.pi/4, np.pi/2, 3*np.pi/4,
                                             np.pi, 5*np.pi/4, 3*np.pi/2, 7*np.pi/4], 
                                            levels=labels.max() + 1)

# we sum over different directions
cooc = np.sum(cooc[:,:,0,:], axis=2)
# and we remove the diagonal
cooc = cooc - np.diag(np.diag(cooc))
        
# adjacency matrix corresponds to the entries > 0.
adjmat = cooc>0
`
Here `labels` is a label image (here an output of a Voronoï tessellation). Obviously, this only works if there are less than 256 objects in the image, due to the limitation of skimage.feature.greycomatrix.

issue_title: Routine to share dataset with user or users
issue_body:
In our upload/sharing model, a dataset is uploaded to an application or organizational user's account, and then meant to be mechanically shared with a designated user or users.

Untracked files:

issue_title: clone_from() with custom environment
issue_body: I want to clone a repository using `Repo.clone_from()` with a specific SSH key. This appears to be possible by setting `GIT_SSH` using `custom_environment()` on a `Git` instance. However, `clone_from()` appears to be creating its own `Git` instance so I am not able to create a custom environment in this case. Would I have to reimplement `clone_from()` to use a custom environment? Or is there some other way of working around this limitation that I am missing?

Check name

Check metadata (I believe this fetches the metadata from the platform. I like it's lazy nature!)

Change file name

Verify the local version has the changed file name

issue_title: ValueError when query string contains "'"
issue_body:
Similar to Issue #404, `oauthlib.common.urldecode` raises a ValueError if the provided query string contains a "'" character.

```
ValueError: Error trying to decode a non urlencoded string. Found invalid characters: set([u"'"]) in the string: 'url=Schr%C3%B6dinger's%20cat'. Please ensure the request/response body is x-www-form-urlencoded.
```

Per [RFC 3986 section 3.4](
https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3986#section-3.4), query strings should be allowed to include the characters defined as `pchar` which in turn allows for characters defined as `sub-delims`.  This includes the "'" character.

issue_title: Refactor 14 lines occurring 2 times in 2 files: genetic1.py, genetic2.py
issue_body:
I've selected for refactoring 14 lines of code which are duplicated in 2 file(s) ([1](https://github.com/PolyJIT/benchbuild/blob/52ec6f984169c158e9517fd6179ad4b6ded27eaf/benchbuild/experiments/sequences/genetic1.py#L264-L281), [2](https://github.com/PolyJIT/benchbuild/blob/52ec6f984169c158e9517fd6179ad4b6ded27eaf/benchbuild/experiments/sequences/genetic2.py#L275-L292)).  Addressing this will make our codebase more maintainable and improve [Better Code Hub](https://bettercodehub.com)'s **Write Code Once** guideline rating! 👍 

Here's the gist of this guideline:
- **Definition** 📖 
Do not copy code.
- **Why**❓ 
When code is copied, bugs need to be fixed in multiple places. This is both inefficient and a source of regression bugs.
- **How** 🔧 
Avoid duplication by never copy/pasting blocks of code and reduce duplication by extracting shared code, either to a new unit or introduce a superclass if the language permits.

You can find more info about this guideline in [Building Maintainable Software](http://shop.oreilly.com/product/0636920049159.do). 📖 
 
---- 
ℹ️ To know how many _other_ refactoring candidates need addressing to get a guideline compliant, select some by clicking on the 🔲  next to them. The risk profile below the candidates signals (✅) when it's enough! 🏁 


----
Good luck and happy coding! :shipit: :sparkles: :100:

issue_title: conda install/create/update taking forever
issue_body:
Hi,

I have never faced any issues before until yesterday when I had to install `blast` using conda. 

I am using `conda install -c bioconda blast=2.6.0` and it is forever in `Fetching package metadata` mode.

 I also tried creating a new env using `conda create -n test` but it is taking forever too. My conda version: `conda 4.2.13`

Also tried updating conda, 
```
$ conda update conda
Fetching package metadata ...packet_write_wait: Connection to 54.226.226.121: Broken pipe
```

Thanks,

issue_title: fiona: pip install works but conda install throws ImportError
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [X] bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior I have, independent of the anaconda environment, a working installation of GDAL. `conda install fiona` appears to be building against the miniconda version of GDAL, which encounters shared libraries with undefined symbols. The error is thrown upon import. However, `pip install fiona` works just fine after I set the `GDAL_CONFIG` environmental variable. I can then import fiona into an ipython session and query shapefiles. This would be just fine if it weren't for the fact that conda does not appear to recognize my pip installation. Consequently, any library I'd like to install that has fiona as a dependency (e.g. geopandas) seeks to also install fiona. I am big fan of letting conda manage dependencies (which it does very well), so while I could hackishly work around this, I'd rather not be in the business of resolving conflicts whenever I need to update any packages that touch fiona. ### Steps to Reproduce Here is the conda failure... ``` [USER ~]$ sudo conda install fiona [sudo] password for USER: Solving environment: done ## Package Plan ## environment location: /home/[USER]/miniconda3 added / updated specs: - fiona The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED: fiona: 1.7.11-py36_3 conda-forge Proceed ([y]/n)? y Preparing transaction: done Verifying transaction: done Executing transaction: done [USER ~]$ ipython Python 3.6.4 |Anaconda, Inc.| (default, Jan 16 2018, 18:10:19) Type 'copyright', 'credits' or 'license' for more information IPython 6.2.1 -- An enhanced Interactive Python. Type '?' for help. [USER]/miniconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/fiona/__init__.py in () 67 from six import string_types 68 ---> 69 from fiona.collection import Collection, BytesCollection, vsi_path 70 from fiona._drivers import driver_count, GDALEnv 71 from fiona.drvsupport import supported_drivers [USER]/miniconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/fiona/collection.py in () 7 8 from fiona import compat ----> 9 from fiona.ogrext import Iterator, ItemsIterator, KeysIterator 10 from fiona.ogrext import Session, WritingSession 11 from fiona.ogrext import ( ImportError: [USER]/miniconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/fiona/../../../libgdal.so.20: undefined symbol: _ZN11xercesc_3_211InputSource11setEncodingEPKDs In [2]: exit ``` Here is the pip success... ``` [USER ~]$ click-plugins in ./miniconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages (from fiona) (1.0.3) Requirement already satisfied: cligj in ./miniconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages (from fiona) (0.4.0) Requirement already issue_title: Better info for PYTHONHOME, PYTHONPATH, LD_LIBRARY_PATH, DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH, other path vars
issue_body:
It's really surprising (or maybe not surprising for those of us familiar at this stage) how many problems are caused by users setting these environment variables and getting "surprising" behavior from `conda` environments. Though these often get discovered at the last moment in a user's issue report, which is bad for time usage and experience on both ends. Would be good if we could catch these earlier and/or avoid them entirely. Here are some rough ideas (some are good, some are scary).

* Listing any of these vars set in `conda info`.
* Warning users if these are already set when activating an environment.
* Warning after installing a package if any of these are set.
* Noting a common fix as unsetting these.
* ((Maybe very) scary option) overriding some of these ourselves.
* Discussion - General info blog post about the the risks of setting these generally (maybe in collaboration with some Linux distro devs and other friends)

Thoughts on these? Other ideas?

cc @kalefranz @msarahan @csoja @isuruf @ocefpaf

issue_title: Conda does not fall back to using soft links
issue_body:
Let me describe a scenario. 

There is a company, called `marvelcomics` and inside that, is a team called `avengers`.  Two members of this team are:

```bash
[shield@earth ~]$ id thor
uid=1004(thor) gid=1002(avengers) groups=1002(avengers)
[shield@earth ~]$ id loki
uid=1003(loki) gid=1002(avengers) groups=1002(avengers)
```

Now, there is a playground called `asgard` where these two guys can play:
```bash
[shield@earth ~]$ ls -ld /asgard
drwxrwxrwt. 4 root root 4096 Jan 10 11:24 /asgard/
[shield@earth ~]$ getfacl /asgard
getfacl: Removing leading '/' from absolute path names
# file: asgard/
# owner: root
# group: root
# flags: --t
user::rwx
group::rwx
other::rwx
[shield@earth ~]$ mount | grep /asgard
/dev/sdb1 on /asgard type ext4 (rw,relatime,seclabel,data=ordered)
```

The user `thor` creates a conda environment on `earth`, inside the playground using conda and installs his favourite tool: `curl`.
```bash
[thor@earth ~]$ wget https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda2-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
[thor@earth ~]$ chmod +x Miniconda2-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh 
[thor@earth ~]$ ./Miniconda2-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -b -p /asgard/kingdom
[thor@earth ~]$ source /asgard/kingdom/bin/activate
(root) [thor@earth ~]$ conda install curl
```

The user `loki` tries to steal this kingdom:
```bash
[loki@earth ~]$ source /asgard/kingdom/bin/activate 
(root) [loki@earth ~]$ conda config --add envs_dirs /asgard/loki
(root) [loki@earth ~]$ conda create -n stealth curl
Fetching package metadata .......
Solving package specifications: ..........

Package plan for installation in environment /asgard/loki/stealth:

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    curl:    7.49.0-1
    openssl: 1.0.2j-0 (soft-link)
    zlib:    1.2.8-3  (soft-link)

Proceed ([y]/n)? y

Linking packages ...
WARNING conda.lock:touch(53): Failed to create lock, do not run conda in parallel processes [errno 13]
WARNING conda.lock:touch(53): Failed to create lock, do not run conda in parallel processes [errno 13]
WARNING conda.lock:touch(53): Failed to create lock, do not run conda in parallel processes [errno 13]


CondaOSError: OS error: failed to link (src=u'/asgard/kingdom/pkgs/curl-7.49.0-1/bin/curl', dst=u'/asgard/loki/stealth/bin/curl', type=1, error=OSError(1, 'Operation not permitted'))
``` 
Why did `loki` think that he could steal that easily? Because, he had read in conda's docs:

> When allow_softlinks is True, conda uses hard-links when possible, and soft-links (symlinks) when hard-links are not possible, such as when installing on a different filesystem than the one that the package cache is on.

... and he had double checked:

```bash
(root) [loki@earth ~]$ conda config --show | grep allow_softlinks
allow_softlinks: True
```

That's weird. Let's see if `shield` has added any protection mechanisms:
```bash
[shield@earth ~]$ sudo cat /proc/sys/fs/protected_hardlinks 
1
```

Of course there is one, so, unless `loki` has ownership of a file or at least `read+write` permissions on it, he can't steal it. Let's check:
```bash
[shield@earth ~]$ ls -l /asgard/kingdom/pkgs/curl-7.49.0-1/info/index.json 
-rw-rw-r--. 1 thor avengers 274 Jul 21 18:56 /shared/thor/pkgs/curl-7.49.0-1/info/index.json
```

Well, `loki` is an avenger, so he has `read+write` permissions on this file and he indeed *can* create a link to *this* file. And conda is correct till now in assuming that he can... However, the reason why he fails to steal:

```bash
[shield@earth ~]$ ls -l /asgard/kingdom/pkgs/curl-7.49.0-1/bin/curl
-rwxr-xr-x. 2 thor avengers 182238 Jul 21 18:56 /asgard/kingdom/pkgs/curl-7.49.0-1/bin/curl
```

There is no `write` permission on this `inode` for `loki`. And hence, he cannot steal blindly.

This scenario makes me raise some queries:

1.  Why does conda check only `${CONDA_PREFIX}/info/index.json` for determining whether or not it can use `LINK_HARD` for the entire package? In other words, how/why is this file the source of truth?
2.  Is there an option in conda using which I can specify that I want to prefer soft-links over hard-links, while creating new environments (using `conda create -n` or `conda env create ...`),  regardless of whether creating hard-links is possible? Why/Why not?

PATH: /opt/local/bin:/opt/local/sbin:/Volumes/Raptor/miniconda3/envs/production/bin:/Volumes/Raptor/Library/Python/3.4/bin:/opt/pgi/osx86-64/13.2/bin:/Volumes/Raptor/anaconda/bin:/Volumes/Raptor/usr/hyperion/bin:/usr/texbin:/Volumes/Raptor/.gem/ruby/1.9.1/bin:/opt/local/bin:/opt/local/sbin:/opt/local/lib/mysql5/bin:/opt/local/lib/postgresql90/bin:/usr/local/hdf5/ifort/bin:/usr/local/mpich2/ifort/bin:/Volumes/Raptor/usr/Montage_v3.3/bin:/opt/pgi/osx86-64/11.10/bin:/Volumes/Raptor/Dropbox/usr/bin:/Volumes/Raptor/usr/bin:/Volumes/Raptor/Library/Python/3.4/bin:/opt/local/bin:/opt/local/sbin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/opt/X11/bin:/usr/texbin
PYTHONHOME: <not set>
PYTHONPATH: <not set>
```

This is causing issues because when installing h5py, which links against the HDF5 installation in ``/Volumes/Raptor/miniconda3/envs/production/lib/``, if I try and import the module, I get:

```
ImportError: dlopen(/Volumes/Raptor/miniconda3/envs/production/lib/python3.4/site-packages/h5py-2.5.0a0-py3.4-macosx-10.5-x86_64.egg/h5py/_errors.so, 2): Library not loaded: libhdf5.9.dylib
  Referenced from: /Volumes/Raptor/miniconda3/envs/production/lib/python3.4/site-packages/h5py-2.5.0a0-py3.4-macosx-10.5-x86_64.egg/h5py/_errors.so
  Reason: image not found
```

If I manually set:

```
export DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH=/Volumes/Raptor/miniconda3/envs/production/lib/:$DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH
```

then import ``h5py``, everything works.

This suggests that there is a bug in conda and ``DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH`` should be set to the environment's ``lib`` directory.

Running ``export`` manually is not a good long-term solution because it has to be re-run anytime I switch environments.

```
Microsoft Windows [Version 6.1.7601]

Deactivating environment "ftd34"...

issue_title: Command line arguments should require latest version (`=latest` version spec)
issue_body:
When a user types: `conda install packagename` or `conda update packagename`, the expectation is that the latest version of `packagename` will be installed. If the latest version is not installable (e.g. it has unsatisfiable dependencies), but there is an earlier version which is installable, the older version will be silently selected.

e.g. https://github.com/Anaconda-Platform/support/issues/26 (not quite the same issue, but it may have been detected more easily)

The packages specified on the command line should be constrained to *equal* the latest version found by conda, or there should be a way that users can specify that they would like this behavior (i.e. `conda install packagename=latest`).

cc @mcg1969 @srossross

issue_title: app/summary section in meta.yaml not respected in binstar after forced re-push
issue_body:
I pushed a package without a summary to binstar.  Realizing my folly, I added a summary section to my meta.yaml recipe like so:

app:
  summary:  An awesome library that kicks ass

Then I rebuilt and did a

binstar upload --force

to replace the package.  However, the summary is not showing up in the binstar web browse.  Is this intended?

For example, see the 'p4python' package in our channel:

binstar.org/ActivisionGameScience

Thanks!

issue_title: Matplotlib 1.4.3 broken on Windows
issue_body:
On Windows I have installed the latest matplotlib, which is version `1.4.3` build `np110py27_2`. When I try to `import matplotlib.colorbar` I get the error `ImportError: DLL load failed: The specified module could not be found.` on line 63 of matplotlib/mathtext.py: `import matplotlib._png as _png`. This happens with both 32 and 64 bit miniconda. 

I tried downgrading to matplotlib 1.4.1 `import matplotlib.colorbar` works fine then.

issue_title: conda.api.get_index not using the platform kwarg
issue_body: In tracking down a particularly fiddly bug with conda-smithy (https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-smithy/pull/274) I noticed that `conda.api.get_index` is no longer using the platform kwarg - it is now exclusively coming from `conda.config.subdir`. That is fine, but I just wanted to highlight the issue, and raise awareness of the value of being able to get an index for any platform (in testing, and in resolving how an environment should look).

issue_title: Plus signs in signatures are replaced by spaces
issue_body:
Some signatures generated through lti aren't accepted by it because they contain spaces, while the signature generated to confirm the body has plus signs at those locations. I assume this occurs because `urllib.parse.parse_qs` is used in the `lti.utils` module.

For instance, calling `generate_launch_data()` on a `ToolConsumer` instance might give me `fJhMAcT9cEl4uyt3 tM q6QKPcw=` as OAuth signature, but oauthlib generates `fJhMAcT9cEl4uyt3+tM+q6QKPcw=` with the same body and secret.

issue_title: [Feature Request] Add function to return object size
issue_body:
A community conversation started on the riak-user list. The full conversation is [here](http://lists.basho.com/pipermail/riak-users_lists.basho.com/2015-April/017071.html). 

> I'm using the python client (by the way).
> 
> obj = RIAK.bucket('my_bucket').get('my_key')
> 
> Is there any way to know the actual size of an object stored in Riak? to make sure something mutable > (like a set) didn't added up to more than 1MB in storage size.

It appears running `getsizeof()` is not a reliable measurement: 

>Map object:
>curl -I
>> 1058 bytes
>print sys.getsizeof(obj.value)
>> 3352 bytes
>
>Standard object:
>curl -I
>> 9718 bytes
>print sys.getsizeof(obj.encoded_data)
>> 9755 bytes
>
>The standard object seems pretty accurate in both approaches even >the image binary data was only 5kbs (I assume some overhead here)
>
>The map object is about 3x the difference between curl and getting >the object via Python.
>
>Not so sure if this is a realistic way to measure their growth (>moreover because the objects i would need this monitorization are >Maps, not unaltered binary data that I can know the size before >storing it).
>
>Would it be possible in some way that the Python get() function would return something like "obj.content-lenght" returning the size is currently taking? that would be a pretty nice feature.

issue_title: Layer outputs being computed multiple times
issue_body:
Lasagne `Layers` compute their outputs by calling `get_output()` recursively on their parents. When a network has a tree structure, which is usually the case, this works fine. But when a network has a directed acyclic graph (DAG) structure, i.e. some layers get their input from the same layer, this leads to `get_output()` being called multiple times on this layer. As a result the output expression of this shared input layer (and all layers further down) is constructed multiple times.

In terms of performance this is not an issue since all these computations are symbolic anyway. We count on Theano's smarts to notice that these sub-expressions are the same, so it only computes them once at execution time. This works great in most cases, but unfortunately there are exceptions:

* it fails when scan is used (see https://github.com/benanne/Lasagne/issues/17#issuecomment-70317085 and onwards, also https://github.com/benanne/Lasagne/issues/97#issuecomment-70718707). ~~This is because Theano does not seem to merge two scan operations that do the same computations.~~
EDIT: this is wrong, see @craffel's comment below. Theano does merge the scan operations, but it doesn't merge their *gradients*, which is also a problem.

* it fails when layer outputs are nondeterministic (because of e.g. dropout), see #97. Random variates are re-sampled for each call to `get_output()`, so Theano rightly does not merge the sub-expressions here. But of course this isn't what we want.

* there may be other cases that have not come up yet or have not been discovered yet.

If we are able to ensure that `get_output()` is called only once on each layer in a DAG, we can solve this issue. There are a couple of ways to do this:

* memoization: make `get_output_for()` remember the output associated with a given input after it's been computed for the first time. The second time it just returns the remembered output (which will be the same Theano expression). Doing this library-wide may result in some gotchas (people expecting code to be executed that will no longer be executed, etc.), so I don't know if this is a good idea. Making it a per-layer thing that should be enabled or disabled seems like it wouldn't solve the problem adequately (and is hard to implement as well), and also adds a cognitive burden for the user.

* making `get_output()` iterative instead of recursive: currently, `get_output()` calls `get_output()` on the parents and then feeds the result to `get_output_for()`. Instead of recursively computing output expressions like this, we could make this function iterative. We would still have to recurse to grab all the 'ancestors' of the layer (we can use `get_all_layers()` for this), but after that we can ensure that `get_output_for()` is only called once for each of them, even when the network structure is a DAG.

* there may be other ways to solve this, ideas are welcome!

I think I like the second option best so far because the interface would remain the same. I also feel like this might be a less intrusive change than introducing memoization. I have to admit I haven't really thought it through yet, though. We'll also have to think about how this will handle `InputLayers` and such (they don't define a `get_output_for()` method, only `get_output()`).

issue_title: Objective class is confusing

Here is the current docstring:

>- input_layer : a `Layer` whose output is the networks prediction
        given its input

While this technically does indicate that `input_layer` should be the output layer of the network, I think something like this would be better:

- output_layer: The `Layer` whose output will be compared to the target.

Also, is there a reason not to specify `target` in the constructor?

issue_title: [v2 Downloader] requirements with dotted package names crash the library check
issue_body:
#### Command name

`cog install calebj captcha`

#### What cog is this command from?

Downloader

#### What were you expecting to happen?

Downloader should have pulled in the dependencies like normal using pip before installing the cog.

#### What actually happened?

Best guess is that since `wheezy.captcha` is a namespace package with a dot in the PyPI name, it's confusing the check. The `wheezy` module will import successfully when any subpackages are installed, but there is no package named just `wheezy` on PyPI. This doesn't happen when the package is already installed.

issue_title: Snowflake support
Problems hydrating previously unhydrated PortfolioItem objects
issue_body:
BIG REWORDING AS I THINK MY ORIGINAL REPORT WAS CONFUSINGLY WORDED.

These problems arose when WSAPI 2.0 started requiring the full hierarchical entity type name of portfolio items in the REST API instead of the sub type name (i.e. requiring PortfolioItem/Feature instead of Feature.) This is handled fine in the initial request to get an object, but doesn't work when pyral needs to hydrate a previously unhydrated object. This is because the code to hydrate the object that builds a get request  doesn't handle the "/" in the middle of portfolio entity type names.

There are two issues:
1) In entity.py `Persistable.__getattr__` incorrectly extracts portolio item entity names when splitting the self._ref. To construct the entity name to send to the Rally API server it needs to cope with a '/' within entity types ('PortfolioItem/Feature' vs 'HierarchicalRequirement')

This is addressed by first splitting on `WS_API_VERSION + '/'` to get a string containing just the entity name and oid before further splitting that to extract those fields.

2) Although the server wants the entity type to be e.g. 'PortfolioItem/Feature' in the request, it uses 'Feature' as the key in the response content. However, the Pyral class for the unhydrated feature object (in this case) is 'PortfolioItem' so we get a KeyError when we try to extract the response content.

This is addressed by catching the KeyError on 'PortfolioItem', then looping through PORTFOLIO_ITEM_SUB_TYPES, re-raising the original exception if we don't find the contents.

Here's a file that shows the problem (if you have access to my workspace and projects - otherwise just put in your own)
```python
import pyral
import metadata


class RallyError(Exception):
    pass


_rally_instance = None


def get_rally_instance():
    global _rally_instance

    if _rally_instance is None:
        _rally_instance = pyral.Rally(
            server=metadata.rally_auth[u"server"],
            user=metadata.rally_auth[u"user"],
            password=metadata.rally_auth[u"password"],
            workspace=metadata.rally_auth[u"workspace"],
            project=metadata.rally_auth[u"project"])

    return _rally_instance

rally = get_rally_instance()

unhydrated = list(rally.get("PortfolioItem/Feature", fetch=False,
                            query='FormattedID = "F226"'))[0]
hydrated = list(rally.get("PortfolioItem/Feature", fetch=True,
                         query='FormattedID = "F226"'))[0]
print hydrated.FormattedID # this works
print unhydrated.FormattedID # this throws an unknown OID 422 exception
```

issue_title: Mesh routing problem
issue_body:
Imagine the following setup: 3 devices A, B and C. A is in reach of B, but out of reach of C and vice-versa. Create network (leader) on node B, then join A and C to B as routers. Using mesh local addresses ping can pass A->B, B->A, B->C, C->B, but not A->C or C->A. The first showstopper I found is when A wants to send a packet to unknown mesh-local adress, it sends address query to B and AddressResolver::HandleAddressQuery is executed, however no response is sent because neither the mesh-C address matches mesh-B (obvious), nor any child of B exists with mesh-C address. To be more precise B->children list is empty. The reason for this is that A and C are removed from children list when A/C->MleRouter::HandleAddressSolicitResponse is called, their short address is chaged from "child-short" to "router-short", LinkRequest sent to B and B->MleRouter::HandleLinkRequest is executed (A and C are removed from B->children when handling Tlv::kSourceAddress).
Without having access to Thread specification I am unsure how this scenario should be handled. Should A and C be left in the B->children list or AddressQuery go past routers or maybe just pass through the AddressQuerry to other routers?
P.S. It seems that multicast addresses are also not retransmitted to other routers/nodes.

issue_title: Any tricks to lazily load dataset when creating Iterator?
issue_body:
Currently OpenNMT-py encounters a memory hogging problem when using large corpus(size of GiBs).

Our (proposed) approach is to divide the large corpus into small ones, and create `torchtext.data.Dataset` for each separately when preprocessing. This approach works fine.

But when do training, this problem emerges again. Because we need to create `torchtext.data.Iterator`, which takes `dataset` as argument. Two possible approaches:

1)  Coalesce multiple datasets into one and pass to `torchtext.data.Iterator`. This approach requires all
    datasets loaded at the same time, so the memory hogging problem comes back.
2)  Create  each `torchtext.data.Iterator` for each `dataset`, and make an iterator chain to iterate over all data.  This actually also requires dataset to be loaded when the Iterator is created, unless we postpone the creation of `Iterator`. And it is hard to iterate across the dataset boundary, because our current __iter__ yields a wrapper `Batch`.

Any tricks to tackle this problem?

issue_title: Versioning of models/views
issue_body:
#1228 is leading to changes in how we deal with model and view version numbers. Here is a proposal:


* Each model has at least 6 attributes:
  * `_model_module`, `_model_module_version`, and `_model_name` describe a model specification, i.e., a documented list of attributes of the model state. The version number is a semver version string. Typically the version number will only have major version bumps (i.e., any change to a model specification is considered a breaking change). The `_model_module` is *not* necessarily tied to a specific package name. For example, the core Jupyter widgets would have a `_model_module` of `JupyterWidgets`. This allows a package to evolve independent of the actual attribute specification.
  * `_view_module`, `_view_module_version`, `_view_name` describe a view that understands the model specification and version. The view module is also not necessarily a package name (for example, core Jupyter widgets have a `_view_module` of `JupyterWidgets`.
* The view implementation used will automatically check to make sure that it understands the model specification at runtime. Thus the `_view_module_version` will normally default to `'*'` (i.e., load a version of the view that implements the model specification). If the view loaded does not support the model specification, an error is logged and a basic view is displayed with an error message.

In practice, third party widgets likely will have their `_model_module` attributes actually be a package name - especially if they don't have many different things implementing them. We could also keep `_model_module` to mean a package name, and make a new `jupyter-widgets` package that contains the specification for the models.

issue_title: `canonicalize_url` use of `safe_url_string` breaks when an encoded hash character is encountered
issue_body:
`canonicalize_url` will decode all percent encoded elements in a string.

if a hash `#` is present as a percent-encoded entity (`%23`) it will be decoded... however it shouldn't be as it is a rfc delimiter and fundamentally changes the URL structure -- turning the subsequent characters into a fragment.   one of the effects is that a canonical url with a safely encoded hash will point to another url; another is that running the canonical on the output will return a different url.

example:

	>>> import w3lib.url
	>>> url = "https://example.com/path/to/foo%20bar%3a%20biz%20%2376%2c%20bang%202017#bash"
	>>> canonical = w3lib.url.canonicalize_url(url)
	>>> print canonical
	https://example.com/path/to/foo%20bar:%20biz%20#76,%20bang%202017
	>>> canonical2 = w3lib.url.canonicalize_url(canonical)
	>>> print canonical2
	https://example.com/path/to/foo%20bar:%20biz%20


references:
* https://github.com/scrapy/w3lib/issues/5

issue_title: Cannot set default return for mock Popen
issue_body:
I've got cases I'm testing where using MockPopen is really handy, but I don't want to specify the exact command in my tests.

One attempted workaround is to replace `MockPopen.commands` with a defaultdict, but this doesn't work as the command matcher tests `if cmd not in self.commands:`.  defaultdict instances return `False` here for items that have not previously been accessed.

The solution I've ended up using is to replace `MockPopen.commands` with a `MagicMock`

```python
popen = MockPopen()
popen.commands = MagicMock()
popen.commands.__contains__.return_value = True
popen.commands.__getitem__.return_value = (b'out', b'err', 0, 1234, 1)
```

This clearly isn't a very nice approach.

I can see two obvious approaches - one would be to add a method `set_default()` to `MockPopen` which would work in a simiar way to `set_command()`.  The other (less nice) way would be to change the command matcher to directly attempt attribute access, and catch and re-raise a `KeyError`.  This would allow a defaultdict-based approach to work.

I'm happy to work up a pull request if it's likely to get accepted.

issue_title: Limit shown tags by default

$ pierone tags myteam

This can potentially output a very huge lists of different artifacts and versions. Even filtering for an artifact can still be a very big list. By default, one only cares about the latest or the last couple of recent tags.

My proposal is to change the default behaviour of `pierone tags` to only show the last 3(?) versions of each artifact with a command line option to either have another number of versions or unlimited versions:

Command | Result
------------ | --------
`pierone tags` | show last 3 versions of each artifact
`pierone tags --versions=5` | show last 5 versions of each artifact
`pierone tags --versions=all` | show all versions of each artifact

```python
def test_pie_default():

from pyecharts import Pie, Style

issue_title: Ca certificates
issue_body:
Hi there.

Currently pypuppetdb documentation around ssl is little misleading.

I followed your instructions to the letter but could not enable ssl verification.

The issue lay around what ca file was being used to verify the request.

I had installed pypuppetdb into a venv and found that the CA_BUNDLE being used was one within the venv and not the system ca file.

If I passed the ca file explicitly "ssl_verify='/usr/local/share/ca-certificates/ca.crt'," then I was able to verify.

I guess there is two things wrong with the documentation.

The docs make no reference to being able to supply the path to the ca file although the requests docs do, also it would probably aid people setting this up to mention that it may not default to the system ca path depending on how they have installed pypuppetdb.

Anyway you have a great library which has aided me using puppetboard, I hope my feedback was helpful.

issue_title: [rllib] Deadlock error when running ES.
issue_body:
When running 

```
python ray/python/ray/rllib/train.py --redis-address=172.31.7.72:6379 --env=Humanoid-v1 --run=ES --config='{"episodes_per_batch": 1000, "timesteps_per_batch": 10000, "num_workers": 400}'
```

on a cluster (100 machines), I see

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/ubuntu/ray/python/ray/worker.py", line 1720, in fetch_and_execute_function_to_run
    function = pickle.loads(serialized_function)
  File "/home/ubuntu/ray/python/ray/rllib/__init__.py", line 17, in <module>
    _register_all()
  File "/home/ubuntu/ray/python/ray/rllib/__init__.py", line 14, in _register_all
    register_trainable(key, get_agent_class(key))
  File "/home/ubuntu/ray/python/ray/rllib/agent.py", line 229, in get_agent_class
    from ray.rllib import es
  File "/home/ubuntu/ray/python/ray/rllib/es/__init__.py", line 1, in <module>
    from ray.rllib.es.es import (ESAgent, DEFAULT_CONFIG)
  File "/home/ubuntu/ray/python/ray/rllib/es/es.py", line 19, in <module>
    from ray.rllib.es import policies
  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap>", line 968, in _find_and_load
  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap>", line 168, in __enter__
  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap>", line 110, in acquire
_frozen_importlib._DeadlockError: deadlock detected by _ModuleLock('ray.rllib.es.policies') at 139937598221224
```

This likely has to do with recursive imports in rllib, probably related to #1716.

issue_title: Support efficient panda dataframes serialization via shared memory
issue_body:
If a panda dataframe is part of a Python object, it should be serialized efficiently and possible to share the memory between processes via shared memory, in a similar manner as numpy arrays are currently handled.

see also https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/ARROW-1503

issue_title: [rllib] Create rllib an API for learning from offline data
issue_body: There are a lot of situations where you have tons and tons of offline data and you want to learn a policy from this, but you don't actually have a simulator for doing on-policy learning. Q learning and similar approaches are a natural fit in this setting, and it'd be nice for RLlib to support this out of the box (e.g., instead of providing agents that take an environment and output a policy, provide agents that take a dataset and output a policy).

issue_title: Segfault in ray.get
issue_body:
The following script seems to crash in ray.get:

```python
# The goal of this exercise is to combine a handful of lessons in a single
# example and to get some practice parallelizing serial code. In this exercise,
# we create a neural network and a gym environment and use the network to do
# some rollouts (that is, we use the neural net to choose actions to take in
# the environment). However, all of the rollouts are done serially.
#
# EXERCISE: Change this code to do rollouts in parallel by making an actor that
# creates both the "env" object and the "policy" object in its constructor. The
# "rollout" function should then be a method of the actor class.

from __future__ import absolute_import
from __future__ import division
from __future__ import print_function

import numpy as np
import psutil
import ray
import tensorflow as tf
import time

from ray_tutorial.reinforce.env import BatchedEnv
from ray_tutorial.reinforce.policy import ProximalPolicyLoss
from ray_tutorial.reinforce.filter import MeanStdFilter
from ray_tutorial.reinforce.rollout import rollouts, add_advantage_values

from ray_tutorial.reinforce.env import (NoPreprocessor, AtariRamPreprocessor,
                                        AtariPixelPreprocessor)
from ray_tutorial.reinforce.models.fc_net import fc_net
from ray_tutorial.reinforce.models.vision_net import vision_net

config = {"kl_coeff": 0.2,
          "num_sgd_iter": 30,
          "sgd_stepsize": 5e-5,
          "sgd_batchsize": 128,
          "entropy_coeff": 0.0,
          "clip_param": 0.3,
          "kl_target": 0.01,
          "timesteps_per_batch": 40000}


if __name__ == "__main__":
  ray.init(num_cpus=4, redirect_output=True)

  # For a more interesting example, try this with the following values. Note
  # that this will require installing gym with the atari environments. You'll
  # probably want to use a smaller batchsize for this.
  #
  #     name = "Pong-v0"
  #     preprocessor = AtariPixelPreprocessor()

  name = "CartPole-v0"
  batchsize = 100
  preprocessor = NoPreprocessor()
  gamma = 0.995
  lam = 1.0
  horizon = 2000

  @ray.actor
  class Agent(object):
    def __init__(self, name, batchsize, config):
      preprocessor = NoPreprocessor()
      self.env = BatchedEnv(name, batchsize, preprocessor=preprocessor)
      with tf.Graph().as_default():
        self.sess = tf.Session()
        if preprocessor.shape is None:
          preprocessor.shape = self.env.observation_space.shape
        self.policy = ProximalPolicyLoss(self.env.observation_space, self.env.action_space,
                                         preprocessor, config, self.sess)
        self.observation_filter = MeanStdFilter(preprocessor.shape, clip=None)
        self.reward_filter = MeanStdFilter((), clip=None)
        self.sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())
    def rollout(self):
      # Collect some rollouts.
      trajectory = rollouts(self.policy, self.env, horizon, self.observation_filter,
                            self.reward_filter)
      add_advantage_values(trajectory, gamma, lam, self.reward_filter)
      print(trajectory)
      return trajectory

  # Create a simulator environment. This is a wrapper containing a batch of gym
  # environments. The simulator can be simulated with "env.step(action)", which
  # is called within the "rollouts" function below.
  #env = BatchedEnv(name, batchsize, preprocessor=preprocessor)

  # Create a neural net policy. Note that we create the neural net inside its
  # own graph. This can help avoid variable name collisions. It shouldn't
  # matter in this example, but if you create a neural net inside of a remote
  # function, and multiple tasks execute that remote function on the same
  # worker, then this can lead to variable name collisions.
  #with tf.Graph().as_default():
  #  sess = tf.Session()
  #  if preprocessor.shape is None:
  #    preprocessor.shape = env.observation_space.shape
  #  policy = ProximalPolicyLoss(env.observation_space, env.action_space,
  #                              preprocessor, config, sess)
  #  observation_filter = MeanStdFilter(preprocessor.shape, clip=None)
  #  reward_filter = MeanStdFilter((), clip=None)
  #  sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())

  # Note that directly making this function a remote function will give a
  # pickling error. That happens because when we define a remote function, we
  # pickle the function definition and ship the definition to the workers.
  # However, this function uses "policy", which is a TensorFlow neural net, and
  # TensorFlow often cannot be pickled. This could be addressed by constructing
  # "policy" within the rollout function, but in this case it's better to
  # create an actor that creates the policy in its constructor (so that we can
  # reuse the policy between multiple calls to "rollout").
  #def rollout():
    # Collect some rollouts.
  #  trajectory = rollouts(policy, env, horizon, observation_filter,
  #                        reward_filter)
  #  add_advantage_values(trajectory, gamma, lam, reward_filter)
  #  return trajectory

  # Do some rollouts to make sure that all of the neural nets have been
  # constructed. This isn't relevant for the serial code, but when we create
  # the neural nets in the background using actors, we don't want the time to
  # create the actors to interfere with the timing measurement below. Make sure
  # that this code uses all of the actors.

  start_time = time.time()

  # Do some rollouts serially. These should be done in parallel.
  #collected_rollouts = [rollout() for _ in range(20)]
  agent = Agent(name, batchsize, config)

  
  collected_rollouts = [agent.rollout() for _ in range(20)]
  collected_rollouts = ray.get(collected_rollouts)

  end_time = time.time()
  duration = end_time - start_time

  expected_duration = np.ceil(20 / psutil.cpu_count()) * 0.5
  assert duration < expected_duration, ("Rollouts took {} seconds. This is "
                                        "too slow.".format(duration))

  print("Success! The example took {} seconds.".format(duration))

issue_title: Shortcut to create Ray wheel
issue_body:
It would be nice to have a utility that given an Ray commit, packages the commit into a wheel for a specific OS/python version. 

This wheel could be then uploaded onto S3 or something.

Use cases:
1. When modifying C++, moving from testing locally to testing remotely requires me to rebuild on the instance - either each instance or by creating an instance then duplicating it, which is a little annoying
2. When someone has an issue and we want to see if the patch works, it would be easy to have them simply pip install a custom wheel with the patch.

issue_title: Facebook backend not retrieving email
issue_body:
We recently experienced an issue where the response from the Facebook graph API at `/me` just after authenticating didn't contain an email field. It's hard to say if this issue is present for everyone, or whether it's just an issue with our site - in Ruby's `omniauth`, users experiencing the issue seemed to be experiencing it intermittently: https://github.com/mkdynamic/omniauth-facebook/issues/61

Adding `SOCIAL_AUTH_FACEBOOK_PROFILE_EXTRA_PARAMS = {'fields': 'id,name,email,first_name,last_name'}` to our settings made it work.

To avoid this problem in the future, perhaps it would be good to have the `user_data` method make the request with a reasonable default set of fields, including email?

issue_title: handling complex dtypes
issue_body:
This is a request for feedback on a potential enhancement to handle complex data for transforms provided by pywt.  Personally, I work with MRI images where the data is complex and both the image magnitude and phase are often of interest.

Currently one can perform complex transforms via separate calls on the real and imaginary parts, but this is a bit cumbersome:
```python
coeffs_real = pywt.dwt(data.real, wavelet)
coeffs_imag = pywt.dwt(data.imag, wavelet)

reconstructed_data = pywt.idwt(coeffs_real, wavelet) + 1j*pywt.idwt(coeffs_imag, wavelet)
```

Is this too niche a case to be worth supporting here in pywt?  I see three potential approaches in ascending order of the work required, but would be happy for feedback on others:

1. leave it up to the user to handle as above.
2. modify functions such as dwt to accept complex inputs, but run the real and complex parts   independently using the existing C codebase.  This would essentially operate like in the example above, but the data/coeffs input/returned would be complex.  The separate real/imag calls would be transparent to users of the functions.
3. update the C code to support ``np.complex64`` and ``np.complex128`` in addition to the currently supported ``np.float32`` and ``np.float64``.

Even if it is determined not to add complex support, I think we should raise errors on complex input as the current code will just discard the imaginary part.  This does issue a warning ``ComplexWarning: Casting complex values to real discards the imaginary part``, but that could be easy to miss.

issue_title: Add 'sysctl' option support
issue_body:
PR docker/docker#19265 merged 'sysctl' support into docker Remote API as of v1.24, but there seems to be no way to use this feature with docker-py. Has anyone started work on such a feature? If not, I will create a PR for a patch that we're using internally.

The sysctl option expects a dictionary of ```{ <name>: <value> }``` (for example: ```{ "net.ipv4.ip_forward": "1" }```), where the types of both objects are of type string. My patch checks if <value> is not a string type, and if that's the case, it casts it using str(). I originally intended to do the casting in docker-compose (for which I have another patch), but now that I've looked closer at the API I believe it belongs in the API. I'm open to debate on that matter though.

I am writing some test fixtures using docker-py. The docker image I am running is exiting within seconds of starting because the command fails. One of the things this loop does on each pass is to check the `Container.status` property of the container. No matter what, it always returns `created`. In my logic, `created` is an acceptable state for the `Container` object to be in since the `created` state seems to always precede the `running` state. Checking existence of LTM rule in 11.6.0 always returns true
issue_body: An LTM policy can have a rule or set of rules attached to it. When checking the existence of a rule in 11.5.4 and 12.1.0, the behavior is the same. If the rule exists, we return True (200 OK) from the exists() call, False otherwise. In 11.6.0 however, the device returns a 200 OK regardless of whether the rule exists or not. We can fix this in the concrete subclass by attempting a load on the rule before returning.

issue_title: Token verification view
issue_body:
We are thinking of using this library to create an authentication microservice in a cluster of server-side applications.The current implementation is great for when your protected resources are on the same server as your JWT endpoint, but would need some alteration for if these were on separate apps.

The authentication flow I am considering is as follows:  a user would log on from our client-side single page app by hitting an endpoint on the auth microservice (AuthService) that serves the `obtain_jwt_token` view. They would then store their JWT in-browser. If the client-side application needed to request a resource from another server-side application (ResourceService), it would pass the JWT in the header. However, the idea is that ResourceService would not have to verify the authenticity of the JWT itself - rather it would make a secondary HTTP call to AuthService to check if the JWT was valid.

This would mean setting up a second type of view on the AuthService which received a JWT and verified its authenticity. I don't think this would be difficult to do by abstracting out most of the `authenticate` method of the `JSONWebTokenAuthentication` class to give it the flexibility to be used either as a function in a view or as a REST framework authentication class.

I wondered if you think a view like this would be a useful addition to the library, or if it is outside the scope of what you are wanting to achieve?

issue_title: Handling fields that may or may not contain relationship information
issue_body:
I'm not even sure how to pose this question.

I have data like:

```
Dave's Credit Recovery / Assignee / Ed's Tire Shop
Eads Asset Collection as assignee of GASCO
GASCO, LLC
GiantBank as Successor Of 3rd National Slightly Less Giant Bank
```
Dedupe's working great for clustering `Dave's Credit Recovery / Assignee / ANY ASSIGNEE` correctly. I imagine I can train it to do the opposite as well, though I haven't tried. 

But is there any way to get Dedupe to also parse and split the relationship in the text field? It doesn't feel entirely far off from the FuzzyCategorical variable type, but that's not quite right for this job

If this is beyond the scope of dedupe, would you have any recommendations for pre-processing that can handle hundreds/thousands of variations of a core set of relationships that are (sometimes) present in the text string? 

Another approach that I think could work would be training the system twice -- once for the "left side" and once for the "right side" of the relationship. As far as I can tell, the order is always consistent, even if the string expressing the relationship varies in form.
