Better organized examples, and parity between C++ and Python
issue_body:
At the moment, the examples that are implemented are kind-of random, and there is no parity between the Python and C++ examples. I think we should re-organize the example section to make sure that:

1. The examples cover all the functionality of DyNet and demonstrate interesting things that it can do.
2. All examples are simple and canonical. For example, they don't contain anything that would be unexpected based on the models they are implementing (https://github.com/clab/dynet/issues/141).
3. There is parity between the C++ and python examples, so neither language is left out.
4. There are not examples that are overly complicated and hard to maintain.

issue_title: logistic function inconsistent negative outputs
issue_body:
dynet.logistic is supposed to return values in (0,1). Because of numeric issues, [0,1] should be ok. However, it seems that it sometimes return tiny negative values when the input values are large negative values.

I am using Python 2.7.14, dynet 2.0.1, and Eigen 3.3.4. The following code shows weird output:

```
import dynet as dy
import numpy as np

a = [0.0 for x in xrange(30)]
a += [-17.0502529144, -17.0516262054, -17.0403594971, -17.0335273743, -17.0677127838]
a = dy.inputTensor(a)

b = dy.logistic(a)
c = 0.5 + 0.5*dy.tanh(a*0.5)

print b.value()[-5:]
print c.value()[-5:]
```

Outputs:
```
[-5.960464477539063e-08, -5.960464477539063e-08, 3.976178675202391e-08, 4.0034372261743556e-08, 3.86889098535903e-08]
[-5.960464477539063e-08, -5.960464477539063e-08, -5.960464477539063e-08, -5.960464477539063e-08, -5.960464477539063e-08]
```

If I change the 4th line as  `a = [0.0 for x in xrange(100)]`,
The outputs are:
```
[-5.960464477539063e-08, -5.960464477539063e-08, -5.960464477539063e-08, -5.960464477539063e-08, 3.86889098535903e-08]
[-5.960464477539063e-08, -5.960464477539063e-08, -5.960464477539063e-08, -5.960464477539063e-08, -5.960464477539063e-08]
```

So, logistic results seem not consistent. I thought the implementation of logistic function is indeed `0.5 + 0.5*tanh(input*0.5)`, but not always.

issue_title: fatal error: mm_malloc.h: No such file or directory on ARM64
issue_body:
Hi dynet-users,

I tried to compile the latest `dynet` version on ARM64. I got the following error message:

```bash
 20%] Building CXX object dynet/CMakeFiles/dynet.dir/mem.cc.o
/media/ubuntu/speicher/repositories/dynet/dynet/mem.cc:13:24: fatal error: mm_malloc.h: No such file or directory
compilation terminated.
```

I could reproduce this error on two ARM64 systems:

* NVIDIA Jetson TX1 with Ubuntu 16.04 and GCC 5.4.0
* ODROID-C2 with Arch Linux and GCC 6.2.1

I guess it has something to do with the following macro defined in `mem.cc`:

```cpp
#if !_WINDOWS
	#include <mm_malloc.h>
#endif
```

As a temporary workaround I removed the `!_Windows` define and added these lines instead:

```cpp
#define _mm_free(p) free(p)
#define _mm_malloc(a, b) malloc(a)
```

inspired from [here](https://github.com/Microsoft/CNTK/blob/master/Source/Common/Include/ssefloat4.h). But this would lead to a core dump e.g. when executing the `./train_xor` test and other test cases.

But I guess we need a more sophisticated solution here :)

issue_title: Abstract (opaque) data types
CoreML UnaryFunctionLayerParams.Operation Threshold converted wrong ONNX Clip
issue_body:
UnaryFunctionLayerParams.Operation Threshold is f(x) = max(α, x). (https://apple.github.io/coremltools/coremlspecification/sections/NeuralNetwork.html#unaryfunctionlayerparams-operation). That is to say, if the element of x < α，we use α. If the element of x > α, we use x. i.e. our min value is α 

But we convert it Clip() { attribute: max: α) (https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md#Clip) , ONNX's meaning is:
> max : float
Maximum value, above which element is replaced by max

i.e. If the element of x > MaxValue (α)，we will use α. The meaning is opposite of CoreML's threshold function。CoreML‘s threshold meaning is that our min value is α, not max value is α.

issue_title: Rename #PYTEST_VALIDATE_IGNORE_OUTPUT
issue_body:
The keyword `#PYTEST_VALIDATE_IGNORE_OUTPUT` used to ignore comparison of computed and saved output originates from a time when the tool was called 'pytest-validate'. As the name now is 'nbval' (standing for NoteBook VALidation), it seems sensible to rename that keyword to `#NBVAL_IGNORE_OUTPUT`.

We should still allow #PYTEST_VALIDATE_IGNORE_OUTPUT to be used for backwards compatibility.

Must update documentation accordingly.

issue_title: Stripe api_version not customizable for stripe.Customer.list
issue_body:
When calling `stripe.Customer.list(api_key=key, api_version='2015-10-16')` with a custom api_version, we get the following error:

```
InvalidRequestError: Request req_I25zAXmrYWc3Mj: Received unknown parameter: api_version
```

This is using python 2.7 via the CLI.

It appears that the `api_version` param is not correctly passed into the `APIRequestor` constructor at https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/blob/master/stripe/resource.py#L451 . I think it should look like https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/blob/master/stripe/resource.py#L924 . There might be other places where this happens as well.

We are able to work around this by just setting the global stripe api version using `stripe.api_version='2015-10-16'`.

Thank you!

issue_title: Unclear on use patterns: PATH script or local file in repo?
issue_body:
If I understand correctly, when the library is `pip`-installed, [the current setup.py file](https://github.com/twitterdev/search-tweets-python/blob/master/setup.py#L14) copies the executable `search_tweets.py` into the `bin/` dir of the relevant python environment. In my experience, this is so the user can run e.g. `(env)$ search_tweets.py` from any location and the stand-alone script will still be on the `$PATH`. However the current `search_tweets.py` doesn't have the `#!` line so it leads to some unexpected errors - in both my case and [this SO post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/18198478/python-import-error-after-making-program-executable?utm_medium=organic&utm_source=google_rich_qa&utm_campaign=google_rich_qa), the resulting output comes from ImageMagik (of all places...). 

[In the README](https://github.com/twitterdev/search-tweets-python#using-the-comand-line-application), the user is instructed to run the *local, repo* file as `(env)$ python search_tweets.py` from the `tools/` directory. This requires the user to have the repo cloned locally. 

I think it would be helpful to be more clear about which is the recommended way for the user to run the main `search_tweets.py` script (not when imported as a library). 

My preference would be to enable the file to run by keeping it in the setup.py script and adding the appropriate shebang. Then, we could remove the language about using the repo version, thus removing the expectation that the user has downloaded or cloned the repo. But I'm happy to hear more about the relative trade-offs of the approaches here.

issue_title: Configurable number of processes used for parallel processing
issue_body:
When enabling parallel processing in the scheduler, a `mutliprocessing.Pool` instance is created by the worker [without arguments](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/worker.py#L399). Hence, [the pool uses the maximum number of processes obtained via `cpu_count()`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/multiprocessing.html#module-multiprocessing.pool).

In my typical workflows where I use a local machine at work, I like to have one spare processor for some interactive tasks (e.g. some shell operations, etc). When all processors run at ~100% for let's say 6 hours, interactive working is not that much fun. So I would really appreciate an (integer) option like "max_parallel_tasks" in the [core] section.

Maybe even some sign distinction could be helpful, sth like:

``` python
...
count = cpu_count()
if count > max_parallel_tasks > 0:
    n_processes = max_parallel_tasks
elif -count < max_parallel_tasks < 0:
    n_processes = count + max_parallel_tasks # max_parallel_tasks is negative here
else:
    n_processes = count

pool = multiprocessing.Pool(n_processes)
...
```

(In that case I would choose `max_parallel_tasks: -1`)

Off-topic: Nice job guys!

issue_title: proxy_server incorrectly assumes bare POST CONTENT_TYPE
issue_body:
proxy_server.py assumes a bare CONTENT_TYPE of ```application/x-www-form-urlencoded``` or ```application/json``` to correctly unpack POST data. My test applicatoin (Chrome Postman extension) sends POST requests with ```CONTENT_TYPE: application/x-www-form-urlencoded; charset=UTF-8```, wich will fail the test at line 39. The correct way to check would be:

```if "application/x-www-form-urlencoded" in environ["CONTENT_TYPE"]:```

and at line 41:

```elif "application/json" in environ["CONTENT_TYPE"]:```

I can now correctly use the built-in oAuth 2.0 auth flow in Postman.

issue_title: Unfreezef and Restart option for FEFF calculation
issue_body: Current FEFF nonconvergence error correction relies on the adjusting of the maximum iterations and convergence accelerator factor. An alternative option, i.e. RESTART card, could be implemented for more robust error correction. If RESTART is specified, FEFF will start the SCF calculation of the potentials from an existing "pot.bin" file, this way one can continue an earlier SCF calculation and saves SCF potential calculation time.

issue_title: Can't support the same query parameter twice in one URL
issue_body:
#### Issue Summary

As far as I can tell, this library doesn't let you make requests to URLs which use the same query parameter key more than once, such as `http://example.com?bar=foo&bar=baz`. This is a problem because many HTTP APIs use this pattern, including SendGrid's own v3 endpoint `/subusers/reputations`. It might be good if you could pass either a dict or a list of two-tuples.

#### Steps to Reproduce

Look at python_http_client.client.Client._build_url. `query_params` is expected to be a dict, or at least something on which you can call `items`.

#### Technical details:

* python-http-client Version: discovered on 1.2.3 but looks like it's applicable to master as well (latest commit: master)
* Python Version: 3.6

issue_title: Add a --user flag for certbot to run as non-privileged
issue_body:
Certbot currently needs root for these reasons:

All:
 - Write to /var/log/letsencrypt/
 - (Create and) write to /etc/letsencrypt/
 - Write to /var/lib/letsencrypt

Apache:
 - Write to /etc/apache2
 - Run `apachectl`

Standalone:
 - Bind to port 443

Manual:
 - None

Lots of third-party Let's Encrypt clients were written because people want to use something like manual mode without root. Right now, certbot lets you do that, but it's complicated. You have to set `--work-dir`, `--conf-dir`, and `--log-dir`. I propose that we add a `--user` flag, or `--no-root` or `--unprivileged` or similar. That would default those three log files to point to `$HOME/certbot/work`, `$HOME/certbot/`, and `$HOME/certbot/logs`, respectively. This would also allow standalone mode to work as non-root, so long as the user was granted the privilege to bind 443.

Slight disadvantage: The keys wind up owned by non-root. But I think that's fine.

In other words, the v0.22.1 release was made at 2fdfdcdc4b5210e9e147d26179a3cfe13090e8f2 but it seems like it should have been made at c5eae8942d53b48b8715d84abed9a44b82cdd51b. Maybe create a new release?

issue_title: KeyError when calling create_tag()
issue_body:
I reviewed the [unit tests for create_tag()](https://github.com/libgit2/pygit2/blob/70edbf256a49dd8a4b8cc16cfe41ae0e2a9a4cc5/test/test_tag.py#L77-L78) and I believe that I have the usage correct. Every time I call `create_tag()` and pass in a valid commit ID it raises a KeyError. `Repository.__get__item()` has no problem finding the commit. Here's a quick ipython session:

```
In [1]: import pygit2
In [2]: repo = pygit2.Repository('.')
In [3]: repo['1ab6ad6606c10698622f771c679bbc7851596878']
Out[3]: <_pygit2.Commit at 0x1096797f0>
In [4]: repo.create_tag('tag1', '1ab6ad6606c10698622f771c679bbc7851596878', pygit2.GIT_OBJ_BLOB, pygit2.Signature('John Doe', 'jdoe@example.com', 12347, 0), 'message')
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
KeyError                                  Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-4-00552c301b3d> in <module>()
----> 1 repo.create_tag('tag1', '1ab6ad6606c10698622f771c679bbc7851596878', pygit2.GIT_OBJ_BLOB, pygit2.Signature('John Doe', 'jdoe@example.com', 12347, 0), 'message')
KeyError: '1ab6ad6606c10698622f771c679bbc7851596878'
```

Am I doing this right? Just let me know I need to provide any additional details or examples!

issue_title: Included toctree includes also arbitrary links in the same document
issue_body: Given I have `conf.py` as following (incomplete): ```python from recommonmark.parser import CommonMarkParser from recommonmark.transform import AutoStructify source_suffix = '.md' source_parsers = {'.md': CommonMarkParser} master_doc = 'index' def setup(app): app.add_config_value('recommonmark_config', { 'enable_eval_rst': True, }, True) app.add_transform(AutoStructify) ``` And I have document `index.md` as following (I inserted spaces in between the backticks of the fenced code block so I could actually put it here, but the original document has no spaces there): ```markdown # Dredd — HTTP API Testing Framework ### Supported API Description Formats - [API Blueprint][...] - [Swagger][...] ## Contents ` ` `eval_rst .. toctree:: :maxdepth: 1 Installation Quickstart How It Works How-To Guides Usage: CLI Usage: JavaScript About Hooks Hooks: Go Hooks: JavaScript (Sandboxed) Hooks: Node.js Hooks: Perl Hooks: PHP Hooks: Python Hooks: Ruby Other Languages Data Structures Contributing ` ` ` ## Useful Links - [GitHub Repository][...] - [Bug Tracker][...] - [Changelog][...] ## Example Applications - [Express.js](http://github.com/apiaryio/dredd-example) - [Ruby on Rails](https://gitlab.com/theodorton/dredd-test-rails/) ``` Then the ToC works perfectly for all other documents, but it includes also links from this index page: I tried the `enable_auto_toc_tree` option, but got similar or worse results.

issue_title: gl.projects.get(project_id), if project is an forked project will get http 500 error
issue_body:
## Description of the problem, including code/CLI snippet
gl.projects.get(project_id)

## Expected Behavior
返回指定id的project

## Actual Behavior
    如果 project 是 forked project , 将会出现 http 500

gitlab server production.log：/var/log/gitlab/gitlab-rails/production.log 显示：
Started GET "/api/v4/projects/264" for 172.16.45.241 at 2018-04-24 14:55:57 +0800
ActiveRecord::RecordInvalid (Validation failed: Visibility level public is not allowed since the fork source project has lower visibility.):

## Specifications

  - python-gitlab version:      python-gitlab (1.3.0)
  - API version you are using (v3/v4): v4
  - Gitlab server version (or gitlab.com):  10.4.5 6b6317a


## Work around
    gl_projects = gl.projects.list(all=True)
    gl_projects_dict = dict((x.id, x) for x in gl_projects)
    project = gl_projects_dict.get(project_id, None)

issue_title: Networks in assigned state are being offered as available
issue_body:
In NSoT 1.0.10, when a Network state is set to "assigned" the `next_network` function will offer the network as available. This is not good and seems to be a regression.

The workaround for now is to forcefully set any networks to state "allocated" and they will no longer be offered as available.

issue_title: Plugin system wishlist
issue_body:
I propose we iterate on the plugin system by creating successively more elaborate examples. [Here are some motivating examples.](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1s8kqh1NSbHlzPCtaI1AbIsLsgGH3bopYbZdM1RzgxN8/edit#heading=h.peealb65mrfb)

As I understand it we want a plugin system that does the following:
- [ ] Allow plugins to insert their own javascript in existing pages, for instance to view content or add a new coach report type.
- [ ] Allow plugins to define new views and urls.
- [ ] Allow plugins to override base templates (for a custom look-and-feel).
- [ ] Has good documentation with examples for all features.
- [ ] Has documentation of core hooks.

Additionally I'm guessing we want the following features:
- [ ] Plugin mixins to cut down on boilerplate. For example, we might have a NavItemPluginMixin that can be thrown in to painlessly add new nav menu items by simply implementing one function.
- [ ] Easily allow an app to register multiple callbacks to a hook.
- [ ] Allows users to define new hooks.

What do you all think?

Package used (Python/R/Scala/Julia):
I'm using Python.

## Minimum reproducible example

This code will calculate the argmax on two three-vectors, returning two integers. The first integer is  correct, the second, which corresponds to the negative inputs, is incorrect. Seems to happen on both CPU and GPU.

```
import mxnet as mx
import numpy as np
data = mx.symbol.Variable('data')
argmax = mx.symbol.argmax(data, axis=-1)
exec = argmax.simple_bind(ctx=mx.cpu(), data=(2, 3), type_dict={'data':np.float16})
exec.forward(is_train=True, data=np.asarray([[1,2,3],[-4,-3,-2]], dtype=np.float16))
exec.outputs
```

This messes up accuracy metrics, for example, when doing half-precision training. You can work around it by adding a large constant offset before taking the argmax, but its obviously a horrible hack that isn't always going to work.

issue_title: Periodic Loss Value when training with "step" learning rate policy
issue_body:
When training deep CNN, a common way is to use **SGD with momentum with a "step" learning rate policy**(e.g. learning rate set to be 0.1,0.01,0.001.. at different stages of training).But I encounter an unexpected phenomenon  when training with this strategy under MXNet.


**That is the periodic training loss value** 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26757001/31327825-356401b6-ad04-11e7-9aeb-3f690bc50df2.png)
**The above is the training loss at a fixed learning rate 0.01, where the loss is decreasing normally**
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26757001/31327872-8093c3c4-ad04-11e7-8fbd-327b3916b278.png)
**However, at the second stage of training(with lr 0.001) , the loss goes up and down periodically, and the period is exactly an epoch**


**So I thought it might be the problem of data shuffling**, but it cannot explain why it doesn't happen in the first stage.  Actually I used ImageRecordIter as the DataIter and reset it after every epoch, is there anything I missed or set mistakenly 
```
train_iter = mx.io.ImageRecordIter(
	path_imgrec=recPath,
	data_shape=dataShape,
	batch_size=batchSize,
	last_batch_handle='discard',
	shuffle=True,
	rand_crop=True,
	rand_mirror=True)
```

The codes for training and loss evaluation
```
while True:
	train_iter.reset()
	for i,databatch in enumerate(train_iter):
                globalIter += 1
		mod.forward(databatch,is_train=True)
		mod.update_metric(metric,databatch.label)
		if globalIter % 100 == 0:
                    loss = metric.get()[1]
                    metric.reset()
                mod.backward()
                mod.update()
```
Actually the loss can converge, but it takes too long.
I've suffered from this problem for a long period of time, on different network and different datasets.
I didn't have this problem when using Caffe.  Is this due to the implementation difference?



Anyone can give some suggestions?

issue_title: Issue Regarding Custom Iterator Using MxnetR
issue_body:
Hi,

I do not understand this code from the [tutorial](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/tutorials/r/CustomIterator.html). It is about the custom iterator using mxnetr.
You can download the original data by typing the code below in your terminal,

```

wget http://pjreddie.com/media/files/mnist_test.csv
```

Below is the original code from the tutorial,
```
csv_iter <- mx.io.CSVIter(data.csv=data.csv, data.shape=c(feature_len), 
                                           batch.size=batch.size)
.self$iter <- csv_iter
val <- as.array(.self$iter$value()$data)

val.x <- val[-1,]
val.y <- val[1,]
```
I took a look at the the original dataset, the first column is the target label, the rest columns are the features. But the code is delete the first row not the first column when defining `val.x`, shouldn't it be something below instead ?

val.x <- val[,-1]
val.y <- val,1]
```

I follow the instruction and it runs smoothly, but I just do not know how that step works.


By the way, I have already used the `h2o` r version package to train my word2vec word embedding vocabulary lookup table. I am wondering if there's any sample code to combine the lookup table and the original raw text into the `custom iterator` using mxnetr, then create the CNN text classification model ?

Michael

issue_title: repeat run constructor function of SymbolBlock when using gluon interface
issue_body:
## Description
when convert `mx.sym.##op##()` to `SymbolBlock` and run forward() during training, i found it will repeat call the constructor function, so if we Initialize some variable in the constructor() and change it in forward(), then it will be wrong because every time after it changed, it will be Initialized again.

give an example here: 
https://github.com/luoyetx/mx-lsoftmax/blob/master/operator/lsoftmax-inl.h#L54-L70

`next_beta_` will be assign as `param.beta * 0.1f` every time before forward(), so we'll never go into https://github.com/luoyetx/mx-lsoftmax/blob/master/operator/lsoftmax-inl.h#L178  

when using no gluon interface(original mxnet symbolic interface), it works well, but if we changed to gluon(SymbolBlock), then no work.

## Environment info (Required)

python
ubuntu16.04
mxnet-v1.0.1

@piiswrong  @sxjscience
